findings.title: Unsynchronized shared Dictionary of decrypted domain master keys raced by relock vs. domain access
findings.defect: A single `ProtectedDomainKeyManager` instance is shared by every protected-data store (KeyMetadataDomainStore, PrivateKeyControlStore, ProtectedSettingsStore, ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore) and by ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator (see AppContainer.swift:680–720 injecting the same instance). It holds decrypted 32-byte domain master keys in a plain Swift `var unlockedDomainMasterKeys: [ProtectedDataDomainID: Data]` with NO lock/actor isolation. The class is a bare `final class` (not even Sendable) reached only through `@unchecked Sendable`, nonisolated stores. `cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey`, `unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)`, and `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` (the latter also zeroizes each Data in place) all run on the cooperative pool because the enclosing store/coordinator methods are nonisolated async. Relock (ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.clearSessionSecrets -> clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys, ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:234) and a store read/cache can execute on different pool threads simultaneously.
findings.invariant: Crown-jewel invariant #4 (key buffers zeroed safely on all paths) and general memory-safety: a Swift Dictionary mutated concurrently with reads/other mutations is undefined behavior; the in-place `protectedDataZeroize()` during `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` races the reader's copy.
findings.failureScenario: On iOS at grace=0, the app is backgrounded while a contacts/key-metadata save is mid-flight: AppLockController.handleAwayEvent bumps awayGeneration and spawns `Task { enterLocked }` -> relockProtectedData -> ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.relockCurrentSession (nonisolated async, off-main) -> clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys() rehashes/empties the dictionary and zeroes the Data values, while the still-running save task calls unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)/cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey on another pool thread. There is no operation-prompt deferral on iOS (that logic is `#if os(macOS)` only). Result: dictionary storage corruption / crash, or a torn Data read yielding a dangling pointer into freed domain-master-key bytes.

votes.reason: The finding accurately reads the current code. ProtectedDomainKeyManager (ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:182) is a bare `final class` with NO Sendable/lock/queue/actor (grep for NSLock/Mutex/DispatchQueue/@MainActor/actor/withLock returns empty across the manager, coordinator, relock coordinator, and KeyManagementService). It holds `private var unlockedDomainMasterKeys: [ProtectedDataDomainID: Data] = [:]` (:186), mutated by `cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey` (`unlockedDomainMasterKeys[domainID] = domainMasterKey`, :339), read by `unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)` (`unlockedDomainMasterKeys[domainID]`, :343), and cleared by `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys` which does per-entry in-place `unlockedDomainMasterKeys[domainID]?.protectedDataZeroize()` then `removeAll()` (:347-350). `protectedDataZeroize()` is `resetBytes(in:)` (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:156). ONE shared instance is injected into the coordinator + all four stores (AppContainer.swift:656/673/687/695/708/716), and `relockProtectedData` = `{ await protectedDataSessionCoordinator.relockCurrentSession() }` (909-910). ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator (:10) is `@unchecked Sendable`, NOT @MainActor, so `relockCurrentSession()` (:208, nonisolated async) and its `clearSessionSecrets()` (:229) → `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` (:234) run on the cooperative pool, off-main. AppLockController is @MainActor (:32); the operation-prompt deferral is entirely `#if os(macOS)` (206-247), so on iOS at grace=0 `handleAwayEvent` bumps `awayGeneration` and spawns `Task { await enterLocked(...) }` (:277) → `enterRelock` (:574) → `await relockProtectedData()` (:580) with no cancellation/quiescence of in-flight domain operations. The stores (KeyMetadataDomainStore:5) and KeyManagementService (:9) are `@unchecked Sendable`, nonisolated; the UI key-gen Task (KeyGenerationScreenModel.swift:220, `Task { @MainActor ... }`) calls `await keyManagement.generateKey(...)`, which hops off-main because KeyManagementService is not @MainActor, so the terminal save (KeyCatalogStore.storeNewIdentity → KeyMetadataDomainStore.save → activeDomainMasterKey → `unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)`, :462) executes on the cooperative pool and can run in parallel with the off-main relock on a different pool thread. No synchronization guards the shared Dictionary. This is an unsynchronized concurrent Dictionary read + structural mutation (undefined behavior) plus an in-place `resetBytes` racing the reader's COW Data copy, directly on decrypted 32-byte domain master keys — implicating crown-jewel invariant #4 (zeroization on all paths). The macOS-only-deferral claim, the single-shared-instance claim, the off-main relock claim, and the nonisolated save path are all confirmed verbatim.
votes.concreteScenario: iOS device, auto-lock grace set to "Immediately" (0), app unlocked with the key-metadata domain committed so its master key is cached in `unlockedDomainMasterKeys`. User starts generating a slow key (e.g. Portable Post-Quantum · High / ML-DSA-87): KeyGenerationScreenModel spawns `Task { @MainActor ... await keyManagement.generateKey(...) }`, and because KeyManagementService is `@unchecked Sendable` (not @MainActor) the `await` hops off-main; generation runs for seconds on the cooperative pool and ends by persisting metadata (KeyMetadataDomainStore.save → persistUpdatedPayload → activeDomainMasterKey → `unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:)` reads/copies `unlockedDomainMasterKeys["key-metadata"]`). Mid-generation the user backgrounds the app. AppLockController.handleAwayEvent runs on the main actor; on iOS there is no deferral (all deferral logic is `#if os(macOS)`), so at grace=0 it spawns `Task { await enterLocked }` → enterRelock → `await relockProtectedData()` → off-main `relockCurrentSession()` → `clearSessionSecrets()` → `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()`, which iterates the dictionary doing in-place `resetBytes` and then `removeAll()`. Pool-thread A (the still-finishing generation save) now reads `unlockedDomainMasterKeys["key-metadata"]` and copies the Data while pool-thread B (relock) structurally mutates the same Dictionary and zeroes the stored Data — an unsynchronized data race: Dictionary storage corruption/crash (a trap under MIE), and the in-place zeroize either races the reader's COW buffer (torn read) or COWs away, leaving an un-zeroed domain-master-key copy alive in the leaked reader buffer, defeating invariant #4. Severity is high (memory-safety UB directly on decrypted key material with a zeroization-hygiene bypass) though it sits at the medium/high boundary: the window is narrow (needs an in-flight domain save/open at the exact instant of an immediate background lock) and the dominant observable is a crash rather than demonstrated key exfiltration.

votes.reason: The concurrency defect is REAL but no security-relevant exploit is constructible. Mechanics confirmed in current code: ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:186 holds `private var unlockedDomainMasterKeys: [ProtectedDataDomainID: Data] = [:]` with NO lock/queue/actor (the only actor in Sources/Security/ProtectedData is ProtectedDataRegistryMutationGate, which guards the registry, not this dict). The zeroizing writer clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys() (:346-351: `unlockedDomainMasterKeys[domainID]?.protectedDataZeroize()` then `.removeAll()`) runs off-MainActor: AppLockController is `@MainActor` (:32) but its relock closure `await`s protectedDataSessionCoordinator.relockCurrentSession(), a nonisolated method on the @unchecked-Sendable coordinator, so it hops to the cooperative pool. The reader unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:) (:342-344) is called synchronously from each store's activeDomainMasterKey during a save/load that is neither cancelled nor awaited by relock. So concurrent Dictionary read+mutate is genuinely reachable — a legitimate robustness bug. But it is NOT an attacker-reachable security defect: (1) the app is zero-network/offline with no untrusted input on this path — the ONLY trigger is the legitimate user backgrounding/resigning during their own in-flight protected-data operation; a physical-access adversary meets a locked app. (2) All outcomes fail SAFE: a crash (app terminates, protected data relocked on relaunch) or a locally-corrupted envelope that fails CLOSED on next read (AES-GCM auth failure -> recovery-needed) — never plaintext, private keys, forgery, or downgrade. (3) Crown-jewel invariant #4 is NOT violated: resetBytes(in:) (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:156) still executes on the relock path, so zeroization is not skipped; a racing reader merely observes its own copy. (4) No key-byte disclosure: the reader's subscript-get ARC-retains the Data payload buffer before `return Data(cachedKey)` (KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:462-463), so the 32-byte DMK cannot dangle/free under the reader — the only buffer at risk of free-during-read is the Dictionary's internal hash storage, i.e. a crash, not exfiltration. (5) MIE / hardware memory tagging (mandatory, constraint #7) converts any use-after-free into a deterministic trap, not exploitable memory disclosure. The claimed "torn Data read yielding a dangling pointer into freed domain-master-key bytes" overstates the mechanism (ARC protects the payload). Net: a rare, self-inflicted, fail-safe crash with no adversary and no victim-facing denial — a hardening/robustness concern worth a defensive lock, but not a security exploit.

The finding maps to crown-jewel invariant #4 (buffers zeroed on all paths), but #4 is SATISFIED by the code as written. `clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` zeroizes each value before dropping it: `for domainID in unlockedDomainMasterKeys.keys { unlockedDomainMasterKeys[domainID]?.protectedDataZeroize() }; unlockedDomainMasterKeys.removeAll()` (ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:347-350). Readers return COW copies and zeroize them — `if let cachedKey = domainKeyManager.unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:) { return Data(cachedKey) }` (KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:462-463, PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:574-575), with the cached original zeroized only by clear. The alleged defect removes/skips NO zeroization call, so #4 is not violated; a race *during* zeroizing is a memory-safety concern, and "no data races / general memory-safety" is not one of the eight crown-jewel invariants. No other invariant is implicated: a torn read yields a corrupted domain master key that makes AES-GCM `open` fail-closed (invariant #1 spirit preserved, no partial plaintext); these are ProtectedData app-data domain keys, not SE-custody private keys (#3 not implicated, no export path); and MIE/MTE (#8) stays enabled and is exactly the mitigation that would trap such a use-after-free, not something the code disables. The finding also overstates reachability: `ContactsDomainStore` never touches the shared dictionary (its `clearUnlockedState()` at ContactsDomainStore.swift:346 is local; grep shows zero `cache/unlocked/clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys` calls), so "contacts save races the dictionary" is factually wrong; and relock is sequential — `relockCurrentSession()` awaits `relockParticipants()` (ProtectedDataSessionRelockCoordinator.swift:16-18, each participant in turn) BEFORE `clearSessionSecrets()`/`clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys()` (ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:213-214, 234), and `KeyManagementService.relockProtectedData()` drains in-flight provisioning via `provisioningCommitCoordinator.waitForActiveCommitsToFinish()` (KeyManagementService.swift:625) with generation commits wrapped in `performCommit` (KeyProvisioningService.swift:253, SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationService.swift:141/302), so the main dict-reading writer is serialized before the clear. The residual theoretical effect (a raced `cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey` insert landing between the zeroize loop and `removeAll`, escaping zeroization) is UB-dependent, non-deterministic, and not demonstrable — its dominant outcome is a crash contained by MIE. No crown-jewel invariant or hard constraint is cleanly violated.
votes.concreteScenario: N/A — refuted. The genuine underlying concern is a latent concurrency hazard (unsynchronized shared Dictionary reachable from multiple cooperative-pool tasks via @unchecked Sendable), but it does not breach an enumerated crown-jewel invariant: zeroization is present on all deterministic paths (#4 met), the fail-closed AES-GCM path preserves confidentiality/integrity (#1/#3 unaffected), and MIE (#8, still enabled) contains the worst case (a crash). This belongs to the concurrency reviewer, not the invariant lens.

findings.title: Torn read of ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.wrappingRootKey (non-atomic Data?) between sync getter and off-main zeroizing mutators
findings.location: Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:16 (var wrappingRootKey), read at :197–:202 wrappingRootKeyData(), mutated/zeroized at :143–:144 and :229–:235
findings.defect: `wrappingRootKey: Data?` is stored on an `@unchecked Sendable`, non-@MainActor coordinator. It is read through the synchronous nonisolated getter `wrappingRootKeyData()` from many nonisolated container closures (AppContainer.swift:689/697/710/718/765/938/1121/1131/1152, CypherAirApp.swift:160/191/240), while `authorizeProtectedDataSession` (:129–:151) assigns it and `clearSessionSecrets` (:229–:235) both zeroizes it in place and sets it to nil — all on the cooperative pool (the coordinator's async methods are nonisolated and the sync getter is called from nonisolated AppContainer code). No lock guards the property. Additionally, because `wrappingRootKeyData()` returns the stored Data by COW copy, the in-place `protectedDataZeroize()` in clearSessionSecrets can copy-on-write and leave an outstanding consumer's shared buffer un-zeroed.
findings.invariant: Memory-safety and invariant #4 (secure zeroization of key material): concurrent non-atomic read/write of a `Data?` value (internal pointer+length pair) is a data race; a torn read can produce a dangling/mismatched pointer, and the zeroize can be defeated by COW.
findings.failureScenario: A protected-domain store closure evaluates `currentWrappingRootKey()` (= wrappingRootKeyData()) on one pool thread at the same instant a lifecycle-driven relock runs clearSessionSecrets() (wrappingRootKey?.protectedDataZeroize(); wrappingRootKey = nil) on another. The reader observes a partially-updated Data struct (stale pointer with new length or vice versa), dereferencing freed/zeroed wrapping-root-key memory — crash or use of corrupted key bytes that then fail domain-key derivation non-deterministically.

votes.reason: The finding's load-bearing premise is factually wrong for this project. It claims the accesses are "all on the cooperative pool (the coordinator's async methods are nonisolated and the sync getter is called from nonisolated AppContainer code)", assuming pre-SE-0461 semantics where nonisolated async hops to the generic pool. But *.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj sets `SWIFT_APPROACHABLE_CONCURRENCY = YES` (9x, all configs) with `SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY = complete` and Swift 6, which enables the `NonisolatedNonsendingByDefault` upcoming feature: a nonisolated async function runs on the CALLER's executor, not the cooperative pool. The project's own code proves this is the operative model — `Sources/Services/KeyManagement/PrivateKeyAccessService.swift:109` states verbatim "`@concurrent` hops this work to the cooperative pool", i.e. `@concurrent` is REQUIRED to reach the pool. The coordinator's async methods (`relockCurrentSession` :208, `authorizeProtectedDataSession` :85, `removePersistedSharedRight` :45) and the stores' nonisolated-async readers (e.g. PrivateKeyControlStore.continuePendingCreate :366) carry NO `@concurrent`, so they execute on the caller's actor. Every caller is @MainActor: AppLockController (@MainActor line 32; drives relock via `await relockProtectedData()` at :580), SettingsScreenModel (@MainActor line 5; drives reset at :406 → LocalDataResetService.resetAllLocalData → relockCurrentSession/resetAfterLocalDataReset), ProtectedSettingsAccessCoordinator (@MainActor line 4, whose `currentWrappingRootKey` is typed `@MainActor () throws -> Data` at :24), and the SwiftUI CypherAirApp. Structural claims are accurate (var at :16 on @unchecked Sendable non-MainActor coordinator :9-10; sync getter :197-202 returns COW copy; assign :144; zeroize+nil :231-232; protectedDataZeroize uses resetBytes at ProtectedDataDomain.swift:156; no lock). But because all reads AND writes of `wrappingRootKey` run on the MainActor serial executor, they never execute concurrently on different threads: `wrappingRootKeyData()`'s synchronous read and `clearSessionSecrets()`'s synchronous mutate each complete atomically w.r.t. the other; MainActor tasks interleave only at `await` suspension points, never mid-synchronous-access. So the claimed torn read of the Data? struct / dangling-or-mismatched pointer / non-deterministic key corruption cannot arise. The finding misreads the effective isolation. The secondary COW-defeats-zeroize sub-point is real only as a general Swift-Data hygiene nuance, is memory-SAFE (COW never dangles), and is bounded because consumers wrap the returned Data in SensitiveBytesBox/locals that self-zeroize in `defer` (e.g. PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:378-381) — it does not produce the claimed high-severity data race.

The structural observation is accurate but not security-exploitable. `ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator` (Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:10) is `@unchecked Sendable`, non-@MainActor, and `wrappingRootKey: Data?` (:16) is read via the sync `wrappingRootKeyData()` (:197-202) with no lock while `clearSessionSecrets()` (:229-235) zeroizes+nils it; the async mutators run off the main actor. A TSan-level race is therefore plausible, but NO attacker scenario produces a security-relevant outcome:

1. No attacker-controlled trigger. Hard constraint #1 = zero network, offline, single-user. No remote actor and no untrusted input (message/file/QR) steers thread scheduling. The race requires a coincidental nanosecond overlap of a user-initiated store read and a lifecycle relock — nothing an attacker supplies causes it.

2. Every outcome is fail-closed or benign. A corrupted wrapping root key flows through HKDF into `AES.GCM.open` (WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordCodec.open, ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:135); AEAD authenticates, so a wrong key yields an authentication FAILURE (fail-closed), never a wrong-but-accepted domain key and never partial plaintext. Used on the wrap path it only writes ciphertext under a garbage KEK (un-unwrappable data loss, not a leak). A torn read causing use-after-free is a local, non-deterministic, self-recovering crash with no attacker benefit.

3. No emission oracle. grep confirms the wrapping root key is never print/os_log/NSLog/export/base64/share'd; consumers only feed it into wrap/unwrap or immediately `resetBytes` it. Even a torn read aliasing adjacent heap memory cannot surface as plaintext — it is only ever consumed as a KEK behind an AEAD.

4. The claimed COW-defeats-zeroize is marginal: `SensitiveBytes.init` does `storage = ContiguousArray(data)` (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:171), an eager separate-allocation copy the consumer zeroizes in its own defer; the coordinator's transiently un-zeroed old buffer is never exposed.

So there is no path to plaintext leak, forged signature, key exfiltration, custody violation, or downgrade. The only formally-matching enumerated outcome (panic/DoS) is not attacker-triggerable and is self-recovering. This is at most a defense-in-depth/correctness hardening nit (add a lock), not a security-exploitable defect.

No crown-jewel invariant / hard constraint is actually violated. The underlying data race is real and reachable, but its consequences are all fail-closed and touch none of the 8 hard constraints.

Reachability (granted): `ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator` is `@unchecked Sendable`, NOT `@MainActor`, with an unguarded `private var wrappingRootKey: Data?` (ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:10,16). Writes run off-main from nonisolated async methods — `clearSessionSecrets()` (:229-235) called by `authorizeProtectedDataSession` (:143,:153), `relockCurrentSession` (:214), `resetAfterLocalDataReset` (:220) — and the assignment at :144. Reads via the sync `wrappingRootKeyData()` (:197-202) are invoked from nonisolated `(() throws -> Data)?` closures inside `@unchecked Sendable` domain stores (KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:5,:469; ProtectedSettingsStore.swift:6,:612) that also run off-main. No lock/actor serializes them. AppLockController.swift:277 (iOS away, grace=0) and :432 (lockNow) spawn detached `Task { await enterLocked(...) }` that do not await the in-flight post-auth fan-out (`await postAuthenticationHandler` at :520), so an off-main zeroizing relock can overlap an off-main read.

Why no invariant is breached:
- Zeroization (#4/#5) is upheld on all LOGICAL paths. `clearSessionSecrets` zeroizes+nils on every path; each consumer zeroizes its own copy — `SensitiveBytesBox(data:)`+`defer { .zeroize() }` (KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:469-472) and `var wrappingRootKey = try currentWrappingRootKey(); defer { wrappingRootKey.protectedDataZeroize() }` (ProtectedSettingsStore.swift:612-615). The "COW defeats zeroize" concern is inherent to any Data-returning key accessor and is covered by that consumer discipline; the only genuinely un-zeroed free requires the exact race window and is retagged by the mandated MIE hardware memory tagging (#7). Not a categorical failure.
- AEAD hard-fail (#3) is untouched: `wrappingRootKey` is the protected-app-DATA KEK, not message-decryption AEAD. A torn/garbage KEK feeds `domainKeyManager.unwrapDomainMasterKey` (AES-256-GCM) → auth tag fails → throws. No partial plaintext.
- Custody non-exportability is untouched: this KEK is derived from a Keychain root secret (`deriveWrappingRootKey`), not an SE non-exportable private key; a torn read exports nothing and there is zero network (#1) to leak to.
- Profile-correct format (#8), secure random (#6), no-secrets-in-logs, MIE (#7) are all unrelated/unaffected.

Worst-case outcomes are a crash (DoS, fail-closed) or a garbage-wrapped domain envelope (local availability/data-loss, fail-closed) — robustness/availability, not confidentiality/integrity/authentication/custody. The finding's "high" security severity (freed/dangling key memory as a breach) overstates a genuine-but-fail-closed concurrency bug.
votes.concreteScenario: No crown-jewel-invariant exploit exists. The most that is reachable (iOS, grace=0): user authenticates → post-auth fan-out begins reading `wrappingRootKeyData()` off-main (opening key-metadata/contacts/settings domains) → user immediately backgrounds → handleAwayEvent spawns detached `Task { enterLocked }` → `relockCurrentSession` → `clearSessionSecrets` zeroizes/nils `wrappingRootKey` off-main concurrently with the read. Outcome is either (a) a torn/nil read → `wrappingRootKeyData()` throws or a garbage KEK → AES-GCM domain unwrap auth-fails → the domain-open operation errors out (fail-closed, no plaintext, no key leaves the process), or (b) a process crash (DoS, fail-closed). Neither surfaces partial plaintext, exports private key material, bypasses authentication, disables MIE, nor reaches the network — so no hard constraint #1-#8 is violated. The residual un-zeroed COW buffer window is retagged on free by MIE. Worth hardening (add a lock or make the coordinator @MainActor/an actor) as a robustness/concurrency fix, but not a security-invariant breach.

findings.title: Certificate-signature verification accepts revoked/expired signer keys and weak-hash (SHA-1) certifications as Valid — no policy, revocation, expiration, or binding checks
findings.location: pgp-mobile/src/cert_signature.rs:452 (eligible_verification_keys); verify calls at :493, :521, :562, :592; FFI entry points verify_direct_key_signature :56 and verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector :74
findings.defect: The certification verification path builds candidate signer keys via eligible_verification_keys() — which clones the primary key unconditionally and any subkey whose self-signature merely *claims* a certification flag (is_explicit_certification_capable, :681) — then verifies with Sequoia's LOW-LEVEL Signature::verify_direct_key / verify_userid_binding (:493/:521/:562/:592). Sequoia's own docs for these methods (sequoia-openpgp-2.4.0 src/packet/signature.rs:3131-3139, 3324-3333) state they 'only verify the cryptographic signature, check the signature's type, and check that the key predates the signature' and that the CALLER must ensure the signer 'is not revoked, not expired, has a valid self-signature, has a subkey binding signature ..., has the signing capability' plus signature creation/expiration time and signature revocations. cert_signature.rs performs NONE of these on the verify path: no StandardPolicy, no revocation_status(), no alive()/expiration check, no signature-expiration check, no policy-driven hash-algorithm rejection. (verify_digest_internal only checks version/salt/DSA and creation-time-predates-key.) Note the asymmetry: the certification GENERATION functions DO enforce revocation and policy validity (ensure_external_certification_certificate_not_revoked, :351; with_policy at :148/:193/:241/:357/:397), so the codebase already knows these checks matter — they are simply missing from verification. The result maps straight through to CertificateSignatureVerificationStatus.valid in Swift (PGPCertificateOperationAdapter.swift:614) with the signer resolved to a 'verified contact', with no mitigating filter on the Swift side.
findings.defect_detail: Bypasses the StandardPolicy that the rest of the app relies on and defeats the OpenPGP revocation mechanism for the certificate-trust (web-of-trust-lite) feature.
findings.invariant: Certification trust / integrity: a certification signed by a revoked or expired signer key, an expired certification signature, or a SHA-1 certification must not be presented to the user as a cryptographically Valid vouch from a trusted contact.
findings.failureScenario: Alice is a user-verified contact. Alice's key is compromised and she publishes a key revocation, which the user merges into Alice's stored contact certificate. The attacker (holding Alice's compromised private key) forges a User ID certification of a malicious key M binding 'bank@example.com', signed by Alice's key. The user imports M plus the certification artifact; the app calls verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector, which re-derives Alice's primary key from the raw candidate-signer bytes and runs raw verify_userid_binding — ignoring the merged revocation — and returns status=Valid with signerIdentity = 'Alice (verified contact)'. The user is led to trust attacker-controlled key M as vouched for by a trusted contact, even though Alice's key is revoked. The same path also accepts a certification whose Signature-Expiration-Time has passed, and (because StandardPolicy is bypassed) a certification made with SHA-1, enabling a chosen-prefix SHA-1 collision transplant of a genuine certification onto a malicious User ID/key.

votes.reason: The verify path genuinely omits the caller-side checks Sequoia requires. `eligible_verification_keys` (cert_signature.rs:452) clones the primary key unconditionally (`cert.primary_key().key().clone()...`, :455) and subkeys via `is_explicit_certification_capable` (:460/:681-695), which only reads `flags.for_certification()` — no policy/validity. Verification then calls the low-level `signature.verify_direct_key(...)` (:493, :521) and `signature.verify_userid_binding(...)` (:562, :592). Confirmed in vendored sequoia-openpgp-2.4.0/src/packet/signature.rs that these + `verify_digest_internal` (:2865) only check v6/salt/DSA, that "Signature (created ...) predates key", and the raw crypto; the doc comment at :3129-3139 explicitly says the caller "must make sure the key is not revoked, not expired ... has the signing capability" and must check "creation and expiration time, or signature revocations." None of that exists on the verify path (no `with_policy`, no `revocation_status()`, no `alive()`). The generation path DOES enforce it (`ensure_external_certification_certificate_not_revoked`, :351; `with_policy` at :148/:193/:241/:357/:397), confirming the asymmetry. Candidate signers are unfiltered: `ContactsVerificationContext.verificationKeys` (ContactsVerificationContext.swift:7) returns every contact public cert, so a merged revocation is ignored; the result maps to `.valid` with signer resolved to a "verified contact" (PGPCertificateOperationAdapter.swift:237). HOWEVER `docs/SECURITY.md:85` documents the feature as introducing "no web-of-trust semantics" and it "never changes manual verification state"; `canEncryptTo`/`isVerified` are independent of certification (ContactKeySummary.swift:28-34), so no encryption-trust elevation occurs. Real defect, limited blast radius → low, not medium.
votes.concreteScenario: Alice is a contact (her public cert is in Contacts). Alice's key is compromised; she publishes a key revocation and the user merges it into Alice's stored contact cert. The attacker, holding Alice's private key, forges a Positive User ID certification over malicious key M's User ID "bank@example.com". The user opens the certificate-signatures screen for M, pastes/imports the forged certification, and runs "User ID Binding Verify". `verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector` re-parses Alice's (now-revoked) contact cert as a candidate signer, `eligible_verification_keys` clones Alice's primary key, and `verify_userid_binding` succeeds on the raw crypto — the merged revocation is never consulted — returning status=Valid, signerIdentity="Alice (verified contact)". The user can save the artifact, flipping M's contact card to "OpenPGP Certified". The same path also returns Valid for a certification whose signature-expiration has passed and (because StandardPolicy is bypassed) for a SHA-1 certification, enabling a chosen-prefix SHA-1 collision transplant. Impact ceiling is a misleading trust-signal badge/record: it does NOT change manual verification state or `canEncryptTo`, so no automatic encryption-trust elevation occurs (docs/SECURITY.md:85 "no web-of-trust semantics"). The fix is to run these certifications through StandardPolicy + revocation/expiration/signature-expiration checks on the verify side, mirroring the generation path.

findings.title: Decompression/output-size bomb in message decryption causes unbounded memory allocation (OOM DoS)
findings.location: pgp-mobile/src/decrypt.rs:285 (decrypt_with_helper: decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)); reached by decrypt_detailed at decrypt.rs:258
findings.defect: The in-memory decrypt path reads the entire decrypted+decompressed plaintext into a single Vec<u8> via read_to_end with no output-size or decompression-ratio limit. Sequoia 2.4.0's Decryptor transparently inflates OpenPGP compressed packets, and a grep of the engine confirms no compression/ratio/size guard exists anywhere. AEAD/MDC is only checked at the END of the stream, so all inflated bytes are buffered before any integrity verdict.
findings.failureScenario: An attacker who holds the victim's freely-shared public key (the app's core exchange model via QR/contact import) encrypts a small (~1-3 MB) message whose payload is a compression bomb (DEFLATE ~1000:1). The victim pastes/opens it and taps decrypt. read_to_end inflates it to multiple GB in a Vec<u8>; on the 8 GB-minimum device this exhausts memory and triggers a Jetsam/OOM kill of the app — a reliable remote-crash DoS. The file variant (streaming.rs:660 zeroing_copy to a temp file) bounds memory but instead fills the disk with the inflated stream before the temp is deleted, a lower-severity disk-exhaustion DoS.
findings.invariant: Availability / robustness against untrusted-input resource exhaustion (no unbounded allocation from attacker-controlled parsing).

votes.reason: The in-memory decrypt path buffers the entire decrypted+decompressed output into an uncapped Vec: decrypt.rs:284-285 `let mut plaintext = Vec::new();` then `if let Err(e) = decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)`. No output-size, ratio, or decompression guard exists anywhere in pgp-mobile/src (grep clean). Sequoia is built with `compression-deflate` (Cargo.toml:19,31; flate2 1.1.9 in lock) and transparently inflates CompressedData during decrypt — confirmed by existing tests test_c2a_9_decrypt_deflate_compressed_message / _zlib (gnupg_message_interop_tests.rs:435-466) which state "Sequoia must decompress it transparently during decryption." The path is reachable for attacker-controlled text messages: lib.rs:789 `decrypt_detailed` FFI -> DecryptionService.decryptDetailed(phase1:) -> DecryptScreenModel.swift:162-164 textDecryptionAction. The only size limit in the flow (ArmoredTextMessageClassifier.maxInspectableFileSize = 256*1024, DecryptScreenModel.swift:149) gates only auto-classification of a dropped file and caps input, not decompressed output, and does not apply to pasted text. large_payload_tests.rs shows the Vec buffering is intentional for legitimate 50-100 MB payloads, so there is deliberately no cap; no doc/comment/test acknowledges the decompression-bomb case, so it is an unhandled gap rather than an accepted risk. Impact ceiling is a recoverable OOM crash with zero secret/data exposure, so I set severity to low rather than the prior pass's medium.
votes.concreteScenario: Attacker imports the victim's freely-shared public key (QR/contact). Using GnuPG or Sequoia they build a literal packet containing a long run of one repeated byte, DEFLATE-compress it (~1032:1), and encrypt to the victim's key — a well-formed SEIPD with a valid AEAD/MDC tag they generated themselves. The resulting armored message is only ~4-8 MB, which inflates to ~4-8 GB. The victim pastes/imports it and taps Decrypt; decrypt.rs:285 read_to_end grows the plaintext Vec past the app's jetsam memory budget on the 8 GB-minimum device, and iOS/macOS OOM-kills the app (reliable remote-triggered crash DoS; recoverable on relaunch, but re-opening the same message crashes again). The file-decrypt variant (streaming.rs:660 zeroing_copy to a temp file) bounds RAM but streams the inflated output to disk; when disk fills, write_all errors and secure_delete_file removes the temp — a lower-severity, self-cleaning disk-exhaustion condition.

findings.title: In-memory decrypt accumulates plaintext with read_to_end, leaking un-zeroized copies through Vec reallocation
findings.defect: decrypt_with_helper() decrypts into `let mut plaintext = Vec::new(); decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)`. read_to_end grows the Vec by repeated doubling; each reallocation copies existing plaintext into a new allocation and frees the old backing buffer WITHOUT zeroizing it. Only the final buffer is zeroized (and only on the error path, line 287); on success the final Vec is returned to Swift (zeroized there by contract), but every intermediate reallocated buffer is abandoned to the allocator still containing decrypted plaintext. This is the shared path for decrypt_detailed() (in-memory message decryption) and decrypt_with_fixed_session_key_detailed() (password decryption), so it fires on essentially every text/message decryption. The streaming file path (streaming.rs) deliberately uses fixed-size Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> buffers and even documents avoiding std::io::copy for exactly this reason, so the project intent is clear and this in-memory path is the inconsistent one.
findings.defect_detail: Invariant 4 (all plaintext buffers zeroed on all paths). Note Zeroizing<Vec> alone would NOT fix it — only the final buffer zeroizes on drop; a fixed-capacity chunked read loop is required, as streaming.rs already does.
findings.failureScenario: User decrypts a multi-KB confidential message. read_to_end reallocates the accumulator several times as it grows; the allocator frees each prior buffer un-wiped. Copies of the decrypted plaintext (including full or near-full content in the last-but-one buffer) persist in freed process heap after decryption, retrievable via a later heap-disclosure bug, forensic memory capture, or core dump, despite the caller dutifully zeroizing the returned buffer.
findings.invariant: Invariant 4: all Data buffers containing plaintext must be overwritten with zeros when no longer needed, on all paths.

votes.reason: Current code at pgp-mobile/src/decrypt.rs:284-293 decrypts into a plain `let mut plaintext = Vec::new(); decryptor.read_to_end(&mut plaintext)`. On error only the final accumulator is wiped (`plaintext.zeroize()`, line 288); on success it is returned (line 293) into `DecryptDetailedResult.plaintext` and marshaled to Swift, which zeroizes by contract. `read_to_end` on an unknown-length streaming Decryptor grows the Vec by amortized doubling; every reallocation that cannot grow in place allocates a new buffer, memcpy's the plaintext-so-far, and frees the old backing buffer WITHOUT zeroing — so intermediate copies of decrypted plaintext are abandoned to the heap on both success and error paths. This shared helper is reached by decrypt_detailed (line 258) and decrypt_with_fixed_session_key_detailed (line 310), so it fires on essentially all in-memory message/password decryption. This contradicts the project's own explicit standard: streaming.rs:3-8 documents that the file path uses Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> intermediate buffers and intentionally avoids std::io::copy/BufReader precisely because their buffers 'are not zeroized', and Hard Constraint #5 requires zeroing all plaintext buffers on all paths. Severity is low rather than medium: it is not directly exploitable (needs a separate heap-disclosure bug, forensic memory capture, or core dump), the residue is the user's own already-decrypted content, and both paths already leave un-zeroizable plaintext copies inside Sequoia's internal buffered_reader stack, bounding the marginal benefit. Note the naive fix (wrapping the sink in Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>) would NOT fully close it, since Zeroizing wipes only the final backing buffer on drop, not the intermediate reallocated ones — a proper fix needs capacity pre-reservation or a chunked zeroing loop like the streaming path.
votes.concreteScenario: User decrypts a multi-KB confidential message via decrypt_detailed (or a password message via decrypt_with_fixed_session_key_detailed). As read_to_end accumulates the plaintext, the Vec reallocates several times (~32→64→…→final), and at each step the allocator frees the prior backing buffer still containing the plaintext-so-far without zeroing it. After the call returns and the caller dutifully zeroizes the final buffer, up to roughly half the message (in the last-but-one buffer) plus smaller earlier fragments persist as un-wiped residue in freed process heap, recoverable via a later heap-disclosure bug, memory forensics, or a core dump — a violation of the all-plaintext-buffers-zeroed-on-all-paths invariant the sibling streaming path explicitly upholds.

findings.title: Passphrase String received at FFI boundary is never zeroized while sibling key material is
findings.defect: export_secret_key() wraps the incoming secret `cert_data` in Zeroizing::new (line 1012) but passes `passphrase: String` by reference (`&passphrase`, line 1013); the owned String is then dropped normally at function exit, freeing the passphrase bytes without zeroizing them. import_secret_key() (lib.rs:1028/1030) has the identical pattern. The same asymmetry (key args wrapped in Zeroizing, passphrase/plaintext left bare) recurs across the password entry points. For export/import the passphrase is the S2K-derived KEK that protects the exported/imported private key, so it is among the most sensitive inputs, yet it is the one field at this boundary left un-wiped. zeroize implements Zeroize for String, so wrapping it in Zeroizing<String> is the trivial, already-used remedy.
findings.failureScenario: User exports a private key under a passphrase. The Rust-side String copy of that passphrase is freed un-zeroized after export; its bytes linger in freed heap. An attacker who later obtains a memory dump or exploits a heap-read primitive recovers the passphrase and can brute-force-free decrypt the exported key file.
findings.invariant: Invariants 4 & 6: passphrase buffers must be overwritten with zeros and never left resident; no passphrase material persists unnecessarily.

votes.reason: The mechanical claim is accurate against current code. In `export_secret_key` (lib.rs:1006-1014) the secret-key bytes are wrapped `let cert_data = Zeroizing::new(cert_data);` (line 1012) but the passphrase is passed by borrow: `keys::export_secret_key(&cert_data, &passphrase, profile)` (line 1013). The owned `passphrase: String` (line 1009) is dropped normally at return; `String::drop` does not zeroize. In secret_transfer.rs:92 `Password::from(passphrase)` receives `&str`, so sequoia's `From<&str>` (`v.to_owned().into()`) zeroizes only its own fresh copy, never the caller's String. `import_secret_key` (lib.rs:1025-1031) is identical (borrows `&passphrase`). This is genuinely asymmetric with every other password entry point (e.g. lib.rs:632 `let password = Password::from(password);`), which MOVE the owned String into sequoia; that move runs `From<Vec<u8>> for Protected` (sequoia mem.rs:136-155) whose body ends `memsec::memzero(v.as_mut_ptr(), capacity)`, wiping the original passphrase allocation. So the siblings are wiped as a side effect of the move, while export/import borrow and leave the owned String resident — a real violation of invariant #4 for the passphrase (which for export/import is the S2K-derived KEK protecting the transferred private key).
votes.concreteScenario: User exports (or imports) a private key under a passphrase. Rust lifts the passphrase into an owned `String`; `export_secret_key`/`import_secret_key` only borrow it (`&passphrase`), so on return the String's heap buffer is freed without zeroization, leaving the passphrase bytes in freed heap. An attacker with a subsequent memory-disclosure primitive or process memory dump could read them. Note the impact is bounded: the trivial suggested fix (`Zeroizing::new(passphrase)`) does not fully close this scenario, because the same passphrase still lingers un-zeroized in the UniFFI transfer RustBuffer and the Swift-side String; and MIE hardware memory tagging raises the bar on the freed-heap read. It is nonetheless a low-cost, correct hygiene fix that restores consistency with the sibling password functions and honors invariant #4.

findings.title: Password-message decryption runs attacker-controlled Argon2 KDF with no memory guard (OOM/Jetsam DoS)
findings.location: pgp-mobile/src/password.rs:569 (skesk.decrypt in derive_candidate); validate_skesk at pgp-mobile/src/password.rs:533
findings.defect: The key-IMPORT path is explicitly protected by Argon2idMemoryGuard (KeyProvisioningService.swift:158-159, parse_s2k_params + a 75%-headroom / 2^31-KiB cap) precisely because Argon2 memory cost can Jetsam the app. The password-MESSAGE decrypt path has no analogous guard. In password.rs::decrypt, each SKESK is handed straight to derive_candidate -> skesk.decrypt(password), which for a v6/SEIPDv2 SKESK6 runs the packet's Argon2id S2K with attacker-controlled m/t/p. validate_skesk (line 533) only checks symmetric_algo/aead_algo support, never the S2K memory exponent. Verified against vendored sequoia-openpgp 2.4.0: decrypt computes m_cost = 2u32.checked_pow(m) and Blocks::new (crypto/s2k.rs:505) allocates m_cost x 1 KiB via alloc_zeroed; m=27 is accepted by both checked_pow and argon2 MAX_M_COST (0x0FFFFFFF), yielding a ~128 GiB allocation that argon2 then writes into.
findings.invariant: Untrusted-input robustness (in-scope): the app's own design intent is that Argon2 memory cost from imported/parsed material must be bounded before allocation (that is exactly what Argon2idMemoryGuard exists for). The message path violates that symmetric expectation.
findings.failureScenario: Attacker crafts a password-encrypted OpenPGP message (SEIPDv2) whose SKESK6 declares S2K=Argon2id with m=27 (2^27 KiB = 128 GiB), t=3, p=4. Victim opens it and taps 'Decrypt with password' and enters ANY passphrase. Before any authentication, PasswordMessageService.decryptMessageDetailed -> FFI decrypt_with_password -> password.rs::decrypt -> derive_candidate -> skesk.decrypt() invokes sequoia's Argon2, whose Blocks::new attempts a ~128 GiB allocation and writes it. On the 8 GB minimum iOS/iPadOS device (os_proc_available_memory ~ a few GB) this immediately triggers a Jetsam termination of the app; the crash is reproducible every time the message is opened (denial of service). No memory guard intervenes because validate_skesk inspects only cipher/AEAD support.

votes.reason: The password-message decrypt path runs attacker-controlled Argon2id with no memory guard, unlike the sibling key-import path. Traced end to end in current code: password.rs::decrypt (154) loops SKESKs → derive_candidate (175) → validate_skesk (533) checks ONLY symmetric_algo/aead_algo support (`if !skesk_v6.symmetric_algo().is_supported()` / `aead_algo().is_supported()`), never the S2K memory exponent → skesk.decrypt(password) (569). In vendored sequoia-openpgp 2.4.0, SKESK6::decrypt (packet/skesk/v6.rs:129) calls `self.s2k().derive_key(...)`, and for S2K::Argon2 (crypto/s2k.rs:192) it runs `config.m_cost(2u32.checked_pow(m.into()))` (196) then `Blocks::new(&params)` → `std::alloc::alloc_zeroed(l)` of block_count×Block(1KiB) (s2k.rs:514-517). The S2K `m` field is parsed as an unbounded u8 (parse.rs:1204: `m: php.parse_u8("argon2_m")?`), and the vendored argon2-0.5.3 sets `MAX_M_COST = u32::MAX` with the explicit comment `// Note: we don't need to check MAX_M_COST, since it's u32::MAX` (params.rs:49,117), so ParamsBuilder::build() does NOT reject large m_cost; block_count ≈ m_cost, so m=27 → 128 GiB and up to m=31 → 2 TiB reach allocation+fill. By contrast the import path calls the guard: KeyProvisioningService.performImportKey:158-159 `let memoryGuard = Argon2idMemoryGuard(...)` / `try memoryGuard.validate(...)` BEFORE importSecretKey. Grep confirms Argon2idMemoryGuard is used at only that one call site; PasswordMessageService.decryptMessageDetailed (61-68) and PGPMessageOperationAdapter.decryptWithPassword (303-322) apply no such check. This is not a documented accepted risk: SECURITY.md §7 (203,205) and Argon2idMemoryGuard.swift:6-8 scope the guard to import by asserting Argon2id is "Not used for routine decrypt/sign," which is true for PKESK recipient-key decryption but overlooks the password/SKESK decrypt path that derives the session key from a passphrase via attacker-controlled Argon2id. The KDF runs before AEAD authentication and regardless of password correctness. The app's own guard doc ("This prevents iOS Jetsam termination") establishes that unbounded Argon2 memory is a real, guarded-against threat, corroborating the DoS on the unguarded path. Impact is availability-only (no plaintext/key exposure), so severity is low.
votes.concreteScenario: Attacker crafts a password-encrypted OpenPGP message: a SKESK6 packet declaring S2K = Argon2id with m=27 (2^27 KiB = 128 GiB), t=3, p=4, followed by a minimal SEIPDv2 container using AES256/OCB (both supported, so validate_skesk and collect_message_context pass). Victim on an 8 GB iOS/iPadOS device (os_proc_available_memory ~ a few GB) opens the message, taps "Decrypt with password," and enters ANY passphrase. PasswordMessageService.decryptMessageDetailed → decrypt_with_password → password::decrypt → derive_candidate → SKESK6::decrypt → S2K::derive_key sets m_cost=2^27, ParamsBuilder::build() succeeds (argon2-0.5.3 MAX_M_COST=u32::MAX), Blocks::new allocates 128 GiB via alloc_zeroed, and argon2 fills/writes the buffer, committing physical pages until the process crosses the Jetsam high-water mark and the OS terminates the app — before any authentication and regardless of whether the entered passphrase is correct. The crash reproduces every time the message is decrypt-attempted. No memory guard intervenes because none exists on this path (only KeyProvisioningService.performImportKey:158-159 guards its Argon2 usage).

findings.title: SR-FIX-05 STILL OPEN — untrusted third-party certification marks a contact "Certified" (trust overstatement, no signer-trust policy)
findings.defect: Certification validity is computed purely cryptographically: verifyDirectKeySignature/verifyUserIdBindingSignature accept the signature if it verifies against ANY candidate signer, and candidateSignerCertificates() = contactService.candidateSignerPublicKeyData() + own keys — every contact key, trusted or not, is an accepted signer. makeCertificationArtifact then stamps validationStatus=.valid, and certificationProjectionStatus() maps any single .valid artifact to .certified, which the UI renders as "Certified" (ContactDetailView.swift:444-445, ContactKeySummaryView.swift:169-170). There is still no separation between 'cryptographically valid' and 'trusted under an explicit signer policy'; a certification signed by an arbitrary untrusted contact key is presented identically to one the user made with their own key.
findings.invariant: Trust/authentication semantics: a 'Certified' endorsement must reflect a signer the user actually trusts, not any cryptographically-valid third-party signature.
findings.failureScenario: Attacker gets their own public key saved as an (untrusted) contact, then supplies a certification signature over victim's contact-key X signed by the attacker key. Victim imports the certification; verification succeeds against the attacker's candidate key, artifact is stored .valid, and contact X is shown as "Certified". The victim reads the badge as an authenticity endorsement of X's key and proceeds to trust a spoofed/substituted key — aiding a key-substitution/MITM, exactly the SR-FIX-05 / CA-09 disposition.

votes.reason: The code behavior is described accurately: candidateSignerCertificates() (CertificateSignatureService.swift:195-198) returns `contactService.candidateSignerPublicKeyData() + keyManagement.keys.map(\.publicKeyData)`, so any contact key (untrusted included) is an accepted signer; makeCertificationArtifact stamps `validationStatus: .valid` (line 234); certificationProjectionStatus maps any `.valid` artifact to `.certified` (ContactSnapshotMutator.swift:890-891); and the UI renders `.certified` as a green "Certified" badge (ContactKeySummaryView.swift:169-170/182-183, ContactDetailView.swift:444-445/458-459), with no own-key-vs-third-party distinction in that badge. However, the claimed invariant ("a 'Certified' endorsement must reflect a signer the user actually trusts") is not a project invariant and directly contradicts the documented design. docs/SECURITY.md:85 states certification "never changes manual verification state, and introduces no web-of-trust semantics"; docs/PRD.md:167 states contact detail "separates local manual fingerprint verification (a local user assertion) from OpenPGP certification (cryptographic evidence...)"; and ContactDetailView.swift:358's footer under the "Trust" section reads "Manual fingerprint verification and OpenPGP certification are tracked separately." The badge is thus framed as cryptographic evidence, deliberately distinct from the actual trust primitive (manual fingerprint verification, shown as its own row). It drives no security decision: `isOpenPGPCertified` (ContactKeySummary.swift:36) is unused; certificationProjection.status is only persisted + displayed; saveCertificationArtifact (ContactSnapshotMutator.swift:562-613) never mutates manualVerificationState; no recipient/encryption gate consults it. No crown-jewel invariant (AEAD hard-fail, format selection, SE non-exportability, zeroization, secure random, log hygiene, no-network, MIE) is affected — the signature is correctly cryptographically verified against the claimed signer and the signer is correctly identified, so there is no authentication bypass, confidentiality/integrity break, or custody issue. This is a deliberate, documented UX-labeling design choice, not a real security defect.
votes.concreteScenario: N/A — no automatic exploit. The "attack" requires the victim to manually import the attacker's key as a contact, manually import the attacker-supplied certification file, click "Verify Signature," and explicitly click "Save Signature." At every step the signer identity is disclosed (ContactCertificationSignerIdentityView shows displayName/fingerprint/source; the saved-history row shows "Signer <shortKeyId>" per ContactCertificationDetailsView.swift:578-587; CertificateSignerSource and isVerifiedContact=false are computed). Saving does not mutate manual-verification state and no code path consumes the `.certified` status for a security decision, so nothing beyond a display badge changes. The app accurately records "a cryptographically valid certification signature from [named untrusted signer] exists," which is the explicitly documented no-web-of-trust semantics — not a trust endorsement.

findings.title: Decrypt-mode summary fold downgrades a bad-signature summary to the softer 'signer certificate unavailable' when an unknown-signer signature follows a bad one
findings.defect: In observe_result, the Err(MissingKey) arm unconditionally sets summary_state = SignerCertificateUnavailable and, unlike the Bad/Err arms, does NOT set summary_stopped. In DecryptLike mode (used by all decrypt paths, decrypt.rs:255/:305 and streaming.rs:585) a prior Bad result (Err(_) arm, :180) sets summary_state=Invalid but leaves summary_stopped=false, so a subsequent unknown-signer result overwrites the summary from Invalid to SignerCertificateUnavailable. The top-level summaryState therefore reports the softer orange 'signer certificate unavailable' instead of the red 'content may have been modified' for a message that contains a genuinely bad signature.
findings.invariant: Summary signature state should not understate the most severe per-signature outcome; a bad/tampered signature in the message must be reflected at least as strongly as an unknown-signer one.
findings.failureScenario: An attacker sends an encrypted message carrying two signatures in order [tampered signature from a known key, signature from an unknown key]. On decrypt, the detailed per-signature list still flags the bad entry red (so this is not 'bad reads as good'), but the folded summaryState becomes SignerCertificateUnavailable (orange, person-with-question-mark) rather than Invalid (red, x-seal). Any UI surface that renders only the summary badge understates the tampering as a mere 'unknown signer' condition. Impact is limited because both states are warnings (isWarning==true) and the per-signature detail remains correct.

votes.reason: The Rust fold mechanic is real but the claimed security impact is not. In observe_result (pgp-mobile/src/signature_details.rs:164-167) the Err(MissingKey) arm unconditionally sets summary_state=SignerCertificateUnavailable without checking summary_stopped, and in DecryptLike mode neither the BadKey arm (:168) nor the catch-all Err(_) arm (:180, which handles BadSignature) sets summary_stopped (only VerifyLike does, :176/:183). So order [Bad, UnknownSigner] folds to SignerCertificateUnavailable. HOWEVER: (1) This is documented intended behavior — the SummaryFoldMode doc (:73-82) states DecryptLike routes "keep following later results until a valid signature wins," i.e. last-non-valid-wins-unless-valid, not most-severe-wins; the test decrypt_like_summary_follows_later_results_until_valid (:336) exercises exactly this. The finding's "must not understate most severe" invariant is asserted by the reviewer, not held/claimed by the code. (2) The claimed impact ("Any UI surface that renders only the summary badge understates the tampering") corresponds to NO real surface. The sole consumer of summaryVerification is DetailedSignatureSectionView.swift:12, rendered only inside `if signatureEntries.isEmpty`. The downgrade requires ≥2 per-signature entries (a Bad plus an UnknownSigner), so signatureEntries is non-empty precisely when the downgrade can occur — the summary badge is never rendered then; instead the ForEach (:14) renders each entry with its own correct state, and the bad entry shows red "content may have been modified" (statusColor .red, xmark.seal.fill). The summary-only surface and the multi-signature trigger are mutually exclusive. Other summaryState readers are SelfTestService.swift:242/:264 (self-generated round-trip, asserts ==.verified; a fold between two non-verified states can never become verified) and PGPMessageResultMapper (passthrough); the tutorial's decryptedVerification is stored but never read for display. (3) No crown-jewel violation: both .invalid and .signerCertificateUnavailable are isWarning==true (SignatureVerification.swift:113-121); neither is the trusted/verified state, so there is no bad-reads-as-good. The per-signature detail is always correct. Net real-world impact: effectively nil.
votes.concreteScenario: N/A — refuted. Even constructing [tampered-known-key sig, unknown-key sig] and decrypting: the detail view renders both entries (bad one red), and the downgraded summaryState is never displayed because the summary badge only appears when signatures is empty. Both candidate summary states are warnings, so no trust downgrade to "verified" is possible.

findings.title: Unbounded PKESK accumulation during Phase-1 recipient scan
findings.defect: The recipient-parsing loops accumulate one KeyHandle/String per PKESK packet into a Vec with no cap, and match_recipients then runs an O(local_certs x pkesk_recipients x keys) nested match. The loop only terminates at the first SEIP packet, so a message with a very large run of PKESK packets before the encrypted container is fully accumulated and cross-matched.
findings.failureScenario: A crafted encrypted file containing millions of PKESK packets (each a handful of bytes) ahead of the SEIP packet is opened for the pre-decryption recipient match (match_recipients_from_file streams from disk). The engine materializes millions of KeyHandles and performs a quadratic match against the user's keyring, spiking CPU/memory. Impact is bounded (input-proportional, requires a large malicious file) and only degrades/soft-hangs the operation, so severity is low.
findings.invariant: Robustness against attacker-controlled entity flooding in packet parsing.

votes.reason: Traced the exact current paths. Accumulation loops: decrypt.rs:165-181 and streaming.rs:946-963 push one KeyHandle per PKESK (`pkesk_recipients.push(rid.clone())`) and break at the first SEIP (`openpgp::Packet::SEIP(_) => break`, decrypt.rs:172-174 / streaming.rs:954-956). The finding's central claim — an "O(local_certs x pkesk_recipients x keys)" quadratic blow-up — is not substantiated by the code. The nested match at decrypt.rs:202-221 / streaming.rs:981-1000 is `for cert in &local_certs { for rid in &pkesk_recipients {...} }`, and `local_certs` is parsed from the `local_certs_data`/`localCerts` argument, which BOTH Swift call sites populate as the user's own keyring: DecryptionService.swift:50 `let localCerts = keyManagement.keys.map { $0.publicKeyData }` and :85 (file path). That outer dimension is small and user-bounded, not attacker-controlled, so the cost is O(|user keyring| x |attacker PKESKs|) — linear in attacker input times a small constant, not quadratic. There is also no amplification: each accumulated KeyHandle (decrypt.rs:169) requires a physically-present PKESK in the byte stream (v3 PKESK with a non-wildcard KeyID is ~12+ bytes; `if let Some(rid) = pkesk.recipient()` skips the all-zero wildcard so zero-cost padding isn't accumulated), and Phase-1 stops at the first SEIP without decompressing the container, so there is no decompression-bomb effect. Each KeyHandle is ~16-40 bytes, so total memory is proportional to the malicious file's size (needing a ~400+MB file to reach ~1GB of handles). Sequoia's PacketParser also caps nesting via max_recursion_depth, precluding stack exhaustion. Threat model: zero-network app, trigger is the user electively opening a large local file; worst case is a bounded, cancellable, input-proportional CPU/memory slowdown ('big files are slow'). No crown-jewel invariant is touched — no partial plaintext, no AEAD/auth bypass, no confidentiality/integrity/custody impact, no memory-safety issue. The claimed 'robustness against entity flooding' is an aspirational hardening nit, not a violated security constraint, and the linear-with-no-amplification profile means it is not a genuine algorithmic-complexity DoS.

findings.title: SE-derived secret copies at the UniFFI boundary are not zeroized on the Swift side
findings.defect: The bridge zeroizes `sharedSecret` and `raw`, but the final `ffiRaw = raw.withUnsafeBytes { Data(buffer) }` copy handed into `P256RawSharedSecret(raw:)` is never `resetBytes`-scrubbed on the Swift side; it lingers in the Swift heap until ARC frees it. The same pattern exists for `ffiShare` in the ML-KEM-768/1024 decapsulation bridges (PGPExternalMlKem768/1024DecapsulationProviderBridge). These buffers hold the raw P-256 ECDH X-coordinate / raw ML-KEM key share derived directly from a Secure-Enclave private operation.
findings.invariant: #4 memory zeroing: all key-material buffers overwritten with zeros on all paths
findings.failureScenario: A device-bound (SE-custody) decrypt runs; the raw SE-derived shared secret / KEM key share is copied into `ffiRaw`/`ffiShare` for the FFI call. Rust copies it into a `Zeroizing<Vec>` and clears its side, but the Swift-side copy is left intact in freed-but-not-overwritten heap memory. A subsequent heap disclosure (e.g. a memory-scraping bug or a core dump before the page is reused) could recover fresh key-agreement material. Exposure is bounded by short lifetime, ASLR, and MIE/MTE, and this is an inherent UniFFI record-copy limitation that the code comments acknowledge (Rust stores in Zeroizing) — so this is a residual hardening gap, not an exploitable break.

votes.reason:
The factual mechanic is accurate but it is an inherent, documented, accepted UniFFI-boundary limitation with maximal feasible mitigation already in place — not an actionable defect.

Trace: PGPExternalP256KeyAgreementProviderBridge.swift L19-31 zeroizes `sharedSecret` (`defer { sharedSecret.zeroize() }`) and the intermediate `raw` (`defer { raw.resetBytes(in: 0..<raw.count) }`), but `let ffiRaw = raw.withUnsafeBytes { buffer in Data(buffer) }` (a distinct heap Data) is returned inside `P256RawSharedSecret(raw: ffiRaw)` un-scrubbed. Same shape in PGPExternalMlKem768DecapsulationProviderBridge.swift:26-28 and ...1024...:26-28 (`keyShare` scrubbed via defer, `ffiShare` copy returned un-scrubbed). So exactly one un-scrubbed Swift Data copy of the SE-derived secret survives per op — the claim's mechanics are correct.

Why it is not an actionable violation of invariant #4:
1. Unfixable within the bridge. The record's buffer must stay live until UniFFI's generated lowering serializes it into the RustBuffer, which runs AFTER the bridge's stack frame returns. A `defer { ffiRaw.resetBytes }` cannot help: `ffiRaw` shares its buffer with the returned record via copy-on-write, so mutating it allocates a throwaway buffer and zeroes that, leaving the record's live bytes intact. The single boundary copy is irreducible; the bridge already scrubs every buffer it controls.
2. Rust re-wraps immediately: keys.rs:195/427/645 comments 'Rust immediately validates and zeroizes it'; external_composite_decryptor.rs uses `Zeroizing::new(classical_ecdh_secret.to_vec())` (and Zeroizing on the KEM shares). The engine side leaves no unzeroized copy.
3. Project explicitly documents this exact class as accepted. docs/SECURITY.md:233: 'UniFFI copies String across the boundary, so the Swift-side copy's lifetime is up to ARC — an accepted platform-wide limitation.' `ffiRaw`/`ffiShare` are the Data analog of that documented carve-out; the code comment at bridge L29-30 acknowledges the copy and the Rust Zeroizing handoff.

Blast radius: the material is an ephemeral per-message P-256 ECDH X-coordinate / 32-byte ML-KEM share, NOT the SE long-term private key (non-exportable; invariant #3 intact). Recovering it compromises at most one message's key agreement, and an in-process heap-scraping attacker who could read freed `ffiRaw` could already read the decrypted plaintext directly — no marginal capability gained. Bounded further by MIE/MTE, ASLR, and sub-millisecond lifetime. The finding itself concedes 'residual hardening gap, not an exploitable break.' Given it is unfixable in-bridge, already maximally mitigated, and matches an explicitly documented accepted platform limitation, there is no actionable defect to confirm.

findings.title: Wildcard/hidden-recipient composite decryption hard-aborts on a classical-component failure that may belong to another recipient's PKESK
findings.defect: In ExternalCompositeDecryptHelper::decrypt, when a PKESK has a wildcard/hidden recipient (explicit_recipient == false), the loop still hard-aborts the entire message if the decryptor records any error for which is_external_operation_failure() is true. ClassicalComponentFailure is classified as an external operation failure, but the classical X25519/X448 shared-secret derivation (core.rs:504-514) runs with the victim's own classical secret against the peer ephemeral taken from that specific PKESK. For a wildcard PKESK the fact that the classical op was 'exercised' does NOT mean the packet is addressed to this key. A contributory-behavior failure (all-zero / low-order X25519 point -> composite_classical::x25519_shared_secret returns Err at composite_classical.rs:167-171) therefore aborts decryption of the whole message instead of being skipped, unlike native Sequoia's decrypt-and-continue behavior for non-matching PKESKs. Only pre-callback InvalidRequest is treated as skippable.
findings.invariant: Availability/robustness of fail-closed decryption: a fail-closed policy must not let an attacker-supplied non-addressed PKESK deny decryption of an otherwise-decryptable multi-recipient message. (No confidentiality/integrity/custody invariant is broken — plaintext is never exposed.)
findings.failureScenario: Attacker crafts a multi-recipient message where an early PKESK is a wildcard (recipient()==None) ML-KEM-768+X25519 (or ML-KEM-1024+X448) composite ESK whose ECDH ephemeral is a low-order / all-zero-yielding point, and a later PKESK is legitimately encrypted to the victim's device-bound PQC subkey. On decrypt, the helper reaches the early wildcard PKESK, derives the classical shared secret with the victim's X25519/X448 secret, gets a contributory failure, records ClassicalComponentFailure, and — because is_external_operation_failure() is true even though explicit_recipient is false — returns Err, hard-aborting before the victim's own valid PKESK is ever tried. Result: the victim can never decrypt a message that contains a PKESK addressed to them (denial of decryption).

votes.reason: The described code path is real and reachable exactly as claimed, but it is a fail-closed availability nit, not a security defect. Trace: external_composite_decryptor.rs:66 sets `explicit_recipient = pkesk.recipient().is_some()` = false for a wildcard PKESK (Sequoia pkesk.rs:64 returns Option<KeyHandle>=None). Lines 67-73 call `.key_handles(pkesk.recipient())`; with None this is an empty iterator, and Sequoia key/iter.rs:411 documents "If the given handles iterator is empty, the set of returned keys is not constrained," so the victim's composite KA subkey IS iterated for a wildcard PKESK and passes the fingerprint filter (75-80). In core.rs:504-514 the classical half runs first: `classical_shared_secret` derives against the attacker-supplied ECDH ephemeral, and an all-zero/low-order X25519/X448 point makes composite_classical.rs x25519_shared_secret return Err (reject at :149-151, derive-fail at :160, or the explicit all-zero guard at :167-171). The `Err(_)` arm collapses every ClassicalComponentError into ClassicalComponentFailure, which is_external_operation_failure() (core.rs:122-131) reports as true, so external_composite_decryptor.rs:100-102 does `return Err(...)` and hard-aborts before later PKESKs. I confirmed Sequoia decrypt_common (pkesk.rs:233) routes an MLKEM768_X25519-algo PKESK into decryptor.decrypt, so a poison packet crafted with matching algo reaches the classical step (needs only a >=1-byte esk field). The asymmetry the finding notes is genuine: unwrap failures (core.rs:527-533) are returned raw and NOT recorded so they fall through (tests.rs:181-195), and InvalidRequest for wildcards falls through (tests.rs:92-112) — only classical/external/response failures abort. WHY THIS IS NOT A REAL SECURITY DEFECT (independent severity = info, below the claimed low): (1) It fails CLOSED — no plaintext, partial plaintext, key, or custody material is ever exposed; none of the 8 crown-jewel invariants is violated (the finding itself concedes this). (2) It requires an attacker who can craft/tamper the raw ciphertext stream, and such an attacker already has trivial, unpreventable DoS (corrupting the SEIPD payload yields the same "cannot decrypt" via AEAD auth failure). The poison-PKESK path grants no capability beyond baseline stream-tampering DoS. (3) The trigger requires a deliberately malformed low-order/all-zero ECDH ephemeral; it never fires for legitimate traffic — even hidden-recipient/throw-keyid messages succeed classical derivation on a valid random ephemeral and fall through harmlessly at the unwrap step (core.rs:527-533, not recorded). (4) Outcome is one message showing a decryption error instead of plaintext — no data loss, no silent wrong result. (5) The behavior is intentional and documented (comments at external_composite_decryptor.rs:88-104 and core.rs:110-131), deliberately prioritizing fail-closed signaling over decrypt-and-continue. There is a legitimate defense-in-depth hardening argument (native Sequoia decrypts-and-continues; the helper could scope the hard-abort to explicit recipients only and treat wildcard classical failures as skippable), so this is a reasonable robustness improvement — but not a confidentiality/integrity/authentication/custody/memory-safety vulnerability and it gates nothing.
votes.concreteScenario: Attacker (MITM on a file addressed to the victim, or any party who can hand the victim a crafted message) builds an OpenPGP message with two PKESKs, both matching the victim's Device-Bound PQC composite KA subkey. PKESK #1 is a wildcard/hidden recipient (recipient()==None) of algo MLKEM768_X25519 whose `ecdh` field is a low-order/all-zero X25519 point (mlkem/esk fields any valid-length bytes, esk >=1 byte); PKESK #2 is the legitimate composite ESK that correctly unwraps the real session key. On decrypt via decrypt_detailed_with_external_composite_key_agreement, the helper reaches PKESK #1 first, iterates the victim's subkey (wildcard => key set unconstrained), derives the classical shared secret against the low-order point -> Err -> ClassicalComponentFailure -> is_external_operation_failure()==true -> `return Err` at external_composite_decryptor.rs:101, hard-aborting before PKESK #2 is ever tried. The victim sees a decryption failure for a message that is otherwise decryptable. Impact is limited to denial of that one message with no plaintext/key exposure, and is not materially different from the trivial DoS the same tampering attacker already has by corrupting the payload. (Identical construction applies to the · High MLKEM1024_X448 path at :338-341.)

findings.title: SQLCipher database and sidecar files receive FileProtectionType.complete only after creation/write, not atomically at create time
findings.location: Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ContactsSQLCipherDatabase.swift:145-157 (open via sqlite3_open_v2) and :44 / :80 (applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles applied after writes)
findings.defect: The contacts .sqlite file (and any -wal/-shm/-journal sidecars) is created by sqlite3_open_v2() with no protectionKey attribute; FileProtectionType.complete is only set/verified at the END of createFresh() (line 44) and replaceSnapshot() (line 80) via applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles(). Because the app carries no com.apple.developer.default-data-protection=NSFileProtectionComplete entitlement, files default to NSFileProtectionCompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication during the write window. This contrasts with plist-domain envelopes, which ProtectedDataStorageRoot.createProtectedFile() creates with .complete atomically. If the process is interrupted (crash/kill) after a sidecar is created but before applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles() runs, that file can persist on disk at the weaker class.
findings.invariant: File-protection-class discipline for at-rest custody data (all ProtectedData files should be NSFileProtectionComplete).
findings.failureScenario: App writes/updates the contacts DB, creating a -journal/-wal sidecar; the process is killed before applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles() runs; the device is then locked. The leftover sidecar sits at CompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication rather than Complete. Impact is defense-in-depth only: the sidecar/DB content is still SQLCipher-encrypted under the DMK, and the DMK/root secret require device unlock + user-presence Keychain auth to recover, so no contact plaintext is exposed by the weaker class alone.

votes.reason: The mechanical observation is accurate but does not amount to a real, substantiated security defect. Traced path: createFresh() line 36 calls open(mode:.createFresh) → sqlite3_open_v2(databaseURL.path, &openedDatabase, mode.flags, nil) at line 147 (SQLITE_OPEN_CREATE, no protectionKey), and .complete is applied+verified only at line 44 (applyFileProtectionToDatabaseFiles → applyProtectionToManagedItemIfPresent → applyAndVerifyFileProtection). replaceSnapshot applies at line 80 after the transaction. No `PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL` is set, so sidecars are transient -journal files (DELETE mode), and createFresh line 33 removes all four candidate sidecars first. Confirmed neither CypherAir.entitlements nor CypherAirMacOS.entitlements has com.apple.developer.default-data-protection, so a non-atomic protection window is technically real. BUT: (1) the domain directory is created at .complete BEFORE the file (ensureDomainDirectoryExists line 34 → createDirectoryIfNeeded → applyAndVerifyFileProtection); iOS Data Protection commonly makes a directory's class the default for files created inside it, plausibly giving the file .complete at creation. (2) docs/SECURITY.md:189 promises SQLCipher sidecars "verify explicit file protection" — which the code does after every write; it never promised cross-interruption atomicity, so no stated invariant is violated in normal operation. (3) Blast radius is nil for confidentiality: any leftover weaker-class file holds only SQLCipher AES-256 ciphertext, whose key (the DMK) is wrapped under a root secret stored kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly + .userPresence (AuthenticationEvaluable.swift:31-48) — requiring device unlock plus biometric/passcode presence, ThisDeviceOnly/non-exportable. (4) On the macOS release target the storage contract fails closed (fileProtectionUnsupported) when the volume can't do file protection. (5) The apply-after-write pattern is a deliberate compensating control — sqlite3_open_v2 owns file creation and has no atomic .protectionKey analog to FileManager.createFile(attributes:) used by the plist envelope path — and is self-healing (replaceSnapshot re-applies on every mutation; SQLite rolls back+deletes stale journals on next open). The finding itself concedes no contact plaintext is exposed. No confidentiality/integrity/authentication/custody/memory-safety impact can be substantiated.

findings.title: Encryption plaintext Vec received at FFI boundary is not zeroized
findings.defect: encrypt() (and the parallel encrypt_binary/encrypt_with_password* family) take `plaintext: Vec<u8>` by value and pass it as `&plaintext` (line 556); the owned Rust-side Vec is dropped without zeroizing when the call returns. The sibling `signing_key` argument on the very same function IS wrapped in Zeroizing::new (line 554), showing the boundary already zeroizes received secrets — plaintext is the omitted one. UniFFI materializes this argument as a fresh Rust Vec copied from the FFI buffer, so a copy of the user's cleartext is left in freed heap after every encrypt call.
findings.failureScenario: User encrypts a confidential message. The Rust-side copy of the cleartext is freed un-wiped after encryption completes; the plaintext remains in freed process heap and is recoverable via a later memory-disclosure vector, even though the produced ciphertext is the only thing that should survive.
findings.invariant: Invariant 4: plaintext buffers must be zeroed when no longer needed, on all paths.

votes.reason: The finding accurately describes current code. `pgp-mobile/src/lib.rs:549` receives `plaintext: Vec<u8>` by value (an owned Rust heap Vec), passes it as `&plaintext` at line 556, and drops it via the default `Vec::drop` at scope end — freeing the allocation without scrubbing. On the very same function, the sibling secret is wrapped: line 554 `let signing_key = signing_key.map(Zeroizing::new);`. The whole encrypt family repeats the omission (encrypt_binary 615, encrypt_with_password 631, and the external-signer variants). Crown-jewel invariant 4 / CLAUDE.md Hard Constraint #5 explicitly names "plaintext" as sensitive data requiring Rust-side `zeroize`, so plaintext is in scope by name. Every other secret-bearing FFI input in lib.rs IS wrapped in `Zeroizing` (secret_keys 795, classical_eddsa_secret, secret_cert, cert_data 493, etc.), and decrypt.rs:288 explicitly `plaintext.zeroize()`s on the error path with a SECURITY comment — so plaintext memory hygiene is an active project concern and this is the sole unwrapped secret input, i.e. an oversight, not a documented exception. Severity is low: not directly exploitable (requires a separate memory-disclosure primitive in the MIE/ASLR-hardened process), and the same cleartext also survives un-scrubbed in the Swift source buffer, the UniFFI RustBuffer, and Sequoia's LiteralWriter buffering, so wiping this one Vec is defense-in-depth rather than a complete fix.
votes.concreteScenario: A user encrypts a confidential message. UniFFI hands Rust an owned `Vec<u8>` copy of the cleartext; after `PgpBridge::encrypt` returns the armored ciphertext, that Vec is dropped and its heap block freed WITHOUT being overwritten (unlike the adjacent `signing_key`, which is Zeroizing-wrapped). The plaintext bytes remain in freed process heap until the allocator reuses the block. If a later memory-disclosure vector (freed-heap read, core dump, swap) is available in the same process, a copy of the user's cleartext is recoverable even though only the ciphertext should have survived the call. Fix parity: wrap `plaintext` in `Zeroizing::new` at each encrypt-family FFI entry, mirroring the sibling `signing_key`/`secret_keys` handling.

findings.title: SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret lacks a deinit zeroize backstop (relies solely on caller defer)
findings.defect: The struct wraps a 32-byte raw P-256 ECDH shared secret in `rawStorage: Data` and only exposes an explicit `mutating func zeroize()`.
