Unlike its sibling sensitive-data wrappers `SensitiveData` (MemoryZeroingUtility.swift:50) and `SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.ClassicalComponent` (SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.swift:161), it has no `deinit` that zeroizes on drop, and `var raw: Data { rawStorage }` / `rawCopy()` can vend additional COW copies. Correctness depends entirely on every current and future call site remembering the `defer { sharedSecret.zeroize() }` pattern (currently done only in PGPExternalP256KeyAgreementProviderBridge.swift:23). A future consumer that constructs the value and returns/throws on an early path would drop the raw ECDH secret to the heap un-zeroed.
findings.invariant: Invariant 4 — all key/shared-secret buffers overwritten with zeros when no longer needed on ALL paths
findings.failureScenario: A maintainer adds a second caller of SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement (or an early-return validation branch) that obtains a SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret but does not wire the defer; the 32-byte ECDH shared secret for a Device-Bound recipient key is released to freed heap memory without zeroization, widening the memory-scan window for that decryption's key-agreement secret. No live leak on today's single call path.

votes.reason: SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret is a `struct SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret: Equatable, Sendable` (SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement.swift:4) — a Swift VALUE type, which cannot have a `deinit` at all (a language constraint). The two "siblings" the finding cites as the standard are reference types: `nonisolated final class SensitiveData` (MemoryZeroingUtility.swift:16, deinit at :50-52) and `final class ClassicalComponent` (SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.swift:147, deinit at :161). They are classes specifically to get deinit-based zeroing; the value carrier uses an explicit `mutating func zeroize()` (lines 23-25) by design, consistent with the file's extensive zeroization comments (lines 81-90). grep confirms exactly ONE production construction site (derive, line 90) and ONE production consumer, PGPExternalP256KeyAgreementProviderBridge.swift:19-23, which does `var sharedSecret = try keyAgreement.deriveSharedSecret(...)` immediately followed by `defer { sharedSecret.zeroize() }` with no throwing code between assignment and the defer — so the zeroize is guaranteed on the current path. The transient CFData in derive is separately scrubbed by `defer { sharedSecret.resetBytes(...) }` at line 89, and if the init throws (lines 12-14) no struct exists. Invariant 4 ("zeroed on ALL paths") is therefore upheld by current code. The finding itself concedes "No live leak on today's single call path" — it is a speculative future-maintainer hazard (defense-in-depth), and its proposed remedy ("add a deinit like the siblings") is not applicable without converting the value type to a class. No current security defect.

findings.title: Composite-generation classical secrets not zeroized if parseKeyInfo throws in material()
findings.defect: `material(from:engine:)` calls `try engine.parseKeyInfo(keyData: generated.publicKeyData)` BEFORE copying `generated.classicalEddsaSecret`/`classicalEcdhSecret` into the returned material struct. The FFI-lifted `generated` (SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate) holds the freshly generated raw Ed25519/X25519 (or Ed448/X448) classical component secrets in its Data fields. On the common success path these buffers are later reset in place by the generation service's defer (COW keeps them as the same backing buffer), but if `parseKeyInfo` throws, `generated` is dropped and its secret Data fields are freed without any resetBytes — no `defer` guards them inside this function.
findings.invariant: Invariant 4 — sensitive key buffers zeroed on error paths too
findings.failureScenario: Freshly generated composite material causes parseKeyInfo to throw (e.g. transient engine/parse failure on the just-built public cert); the raw classical component scalars (half of a Device-Bound Post-Quantum split-custody key) are released to the heap un-zeroed. Exposure is bounded: the classical half alone cannot sign or decrypt without the enclave-resident ML-DSA/ML-KEM component (per POST_QUANTUM.md §3), and the path requires a parse failure on valid self-generated material.

votes.reason: In `material(from:engine:)` (PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeGenerationAdapter.swift:130-145), line 134 `let keyInfo = try engine.parseKeyInfo(keyData: generated.publicKeyData)` runs before the classical secrets are copied out at lines 142-143 (`classicalEddsaSecret: generated.classicalEddsaSecret`, `classicalEcdhSecret: generated.classicalEcdhSecret`). There is no `defer` in `material`, and neither caller has one either: `performGenerateCompositeCertificate` (lines 96-100) and `performGenerateCompositeHighCertificate` (lines 120-124) hold the FFI-lifted `SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate` in a bare `let generated` and `return try material(...)`. The only zeroization of these buffers is the caller's `defer` in SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationService.swift:281-288, which is installed only AFTER `generateCompositeCertificate` returns successfully (`var generated = try await ...`, line 269). If `parseKeyInfo` throws at line 134, that success-path defer is never reached; `generateCompositeCertificate` maps/rethrows (lines 74-76) and the FFI-lifted struct's `classicalEddsaSecret`/`classicalEcdhSecret` Swift `Data` buffers — a heap allocation created by `FfiConverterData.read`, distinct from the Rust-side zeroized copy — are freed by `Data`'s deinit with no `resetBytes`. That violates Invariant 4 (zeroize sensitive key buffers on all paths including errors). No comment/doc/test marks the omission as intentional; the caller's explicit defer and the struct doc-comment ("the caller must ... zeroize the buffers") show intent is to always zero these.
votes.concreteScenario: User generates a Device-Bound Post-Quantum (or ·High) key. Rust builds the composite public cert and returns the freshly generated classical Ed25519/X25519 (or Ed448/X448) private scalars in `SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate.classicalEddsaSecret`/`.classicalEcdhSecret`. Inside `material(from:)`, `engine.parseKeyInfo` throws (e.g. a transient/OOM/internal failure parsing the just-built cert). The function unwinds without constructing the returned material and without any defer, so the two classical-secret `Data` buffers are released un-zeroed, leaving raw private-scalar bytes in freed heap. Blast radius is minimal: not attacker-triggerable (no untrusted input drives the parse), exposure is in-process freed heap only (MIE/MTE-tagged; no disk/log/network egress), and the leaked classical half is independently unusable for signing or decryption without the non-exportable SE-resident ML-DSA/ML-KEM component of the split-custody key.

findings.title: Unchecked left shift on attacker-controlled Argon2 exponent in parse_s2k_params
findings.defect: `let memory_kib: u64 = 1u64 << (*m as u64);` shifts by an attacker-controlled u8 with no bounds check. For m >= 64 this is Rust arithmetic overflow: in debug builds it panics ('attempt to shift left with overflow'); in release builds it is masked to `1u64 << (m & 63)`, producing a wrong, small memory_kib. A small reported value would sail through Argon2idMemoryGuard (both the 2^31 cap and the 75% check), i.e. the guard's memory estimate becomes meaningless for those inputs. RFC 9580 caps encoded_m at 31, and this code trusts sequoia's parser to have validated m, which it does not do at parse time.
findings.invariant: Memory-guard bounds correctness / untrusted-input robustness: parse_s2k_params must return a truthful memory requirement for the guard to be meaningful.
findings.failureScenario: A malicious passphrase-protected key file sets an encrypted subkey's Argon2 exponent m=64. parse_s2k_params computes 1u64 << 64 -> (release) masks to 1 KiB, so Argon2idMemoryGuard treats the key as trivially safe and allows import. In practice the downstream OOM is NOT reached because sequoia independently rejects m>=32 during decrypt (2u32.checked_pow(m) overflows to None -> InvalidArgument), so importSecretKey fails cleanly rather than allocating; thus no OOM in production. Residual impact: debug/test builds panic on parse of such a key, and the guard's correctness relies entirely on a sequoia-internal cap rather than validating its own input. Should be a `checked_shl`/explicit m<=31 clamp.

votes.reason:
The code observation is literally accurate but there is no exploitable security defect. pgp-mobile/src/keys/s2k.rs:38 `let memory_kib: u64 = 1u64 << (*m as u64);` does shift by an unvalidated attacker-controlled u8 (sequoia's wire parser at parse.rs:1204 reads `m: php.parse_u8("argon2_m")?` with no RFC cap). However: (1) pgp-mobile/Cargo.toml `[profile.release]` does NOT enable overflow-checks, so production release builds MASK (`1u64 << (m & 63)`) rather than panic — the panic is debug-only. (2) The masked/overflow range m>=64 is a strict subset of the range sequoia independently rejects at decrypt: crypto/s2k.rs:197 `2u32.checked_pow(m.into()).ok_or_else(...)` returns Error::InvalidArgument for ALL m>=32 BEFORE any Argon2 allocation. Because [64,255] ⊂ [>=32], there is no m for which the guard is bypassed AND the key is actually decryptable — so no OOM/Jetsam is reachable. (3) The genuinely dangerous RFC-valid range (m∈~[20,31], e.g. m=25 → 32 GiB) is computed CORRECTLY (25<64, no overflow) and rejected by Argon2idMemoryGuard (maxMemoryKib=1<<31 plus overflow-checked 75% test); flow order confirmed at KeyProvisioningService.swift:159 validate() then :163 importSecretKey(). The debug panic is not reachable by any current test (keys use m=19; xcframework ships --release). Rust shift-masking is well-defined, so no memory-safety/UB. Net impact on confidentiality/integrity/authentication/custody/memory-safety is nil; this is at most a defense-in-depth hardening nit (prefer checked_shl or an m<=31 clamp), matching the finding's own concession of 'no OOM in production'.</parameter>
</invoke>

findings.title: WCR-03 STILL OPEN — derived wrapping-root-key (top-level KEK) left un-zeroed at domain-provisioning sites and on every dataCopy()
findings.defect: At provisioning, `let derivedWrappingRootKey = try domainKeyManager.deriveWrappingRootKey(...)` returns a plaintext KEK Data; it is copied into a SensitiveBytesBox (which does `ContiguousArray(data)` — a fresh buffer), but the original `derivedWrappingRootKey` local is never zeroized (the defer only zeroizes the box + provisionedSecret). Additionally SensitiveBytesBox.dataCopy() mints a brand-new Data copy on every call (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:174-176/195-197); these copies are handed to stageInitialPayload / persistSharedRight / wrap/unwrap (e.g. PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:122,127,133; KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:145,151) and are never zeroized. So multiple plaintext copies of the wrapping-root-key — the KEK that wraps every domain master key protecting private keys, contacts DB, and key metadata — linger in freed heap memory.
findings.invariant: Hard Constraint #5 (memory zeroing): all Data buffers holding key material must be overwritten with zeros on all paths.
findings.failureScenario: During first-domain provisioning the derived KEK and its dataCopy() clones are freed without zeroing; a later heap disclosure (memory-inspection, core dump, swap on non-MIE surface such as visionOS, or a UAF) can recover the top-level wrapping root key and unwrap every protected domain. Defense-in-depth is defeated; MIE reduces but the invariant is a hard constraint that is violated.

votes.reason:
The claim is substantiated by the current code. At both provisioning sites the derived top-level KEK is left in an un-zeroed heap buffer:

ProtectedSettingsStore.swift:210-216
  210: let derivedWrappingRootKey = try domainKeyManager.deriveWrappingRootKey(from: &rawRootKeyInput)
  212: let wrappingRootKey = SensitiveBytesBox(data: derivedWrappingRootKey)
  213-216: defer { provisionedSecret.zeroize(); wrappingRootKey.zeroize() }   // derivedWrappingRootKey NOT zeroized
PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:91-97 is identical.

- deriveWrappingRootKey returns a fresh KEK Data (ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:210 `return key.withUnsafeBytes { Data($0) }`).
- SensitiveBytes.init deep-copies into a separate buffer (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:170-172 `storage = ContiguousArray(data)`), so the box's storage is distinct from the `derivedWrappingRootKey` buffer.
- `derivedWrappingRootKey` is a `let`, so `protectedDataZeroize()` (mutating) can never run on it, and the defer only clears the box + provisionedSecret. Its 32-byte KEK buffer is freed un-zeroed at scope exit. This is a literal violation of Hard Constraint #5 / crown-jewel invariant (4) for that copy.

Contrast confirms it's avoidable, not intrinsic: on the recurring auth path (ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.swift:139-144) the same `let derivedWrappingRootKey` is COW-shared into `self.wrappingRootKey`, which clearSessionSecrets() zeroizes at relock (line 229-235) — so that path leaves no orphan; the provisioning path deep-copies into the box and orphans the local.

Secondary: SensitiveBytesBox.dataCopy() (ProtectedDataDomain.swift:174-176 `Data(storage)`) mints fresh copies passed as `let` params to stageInitialPayload / readAuthoritativeSnapshot (ProtectedSettingsStore.swift:430-453, 510-518; PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:443-466, 519-531) and to persistSharedRight; those params are forwarded to wrap/unwrap (which zeroize only the derived domain-wrapping key, ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:237-262, not the root) and are never zeroized. These transients are real but short-lived COW artifacts.

No comment/doc/test marks this as an accepted risk; no in-code path zeroizes the `let` local.

Severity is low: this is a defense-in-depth memory-hygiene gap, not an in-band confidentiality break. It is not reachable by any input-driven code path; realizing it needs a separate heap-disclosure primitive, and during an unlocked session that same primitive already exposes the live KEK (coordinator wrappingRootKey) and live cached domain master keys (unlockedDomainMasterKeys), making the incremental exposure marginal (post-relock residual window on one-time provisioning buffers). MIE/MTE further blunts the UAF vector on shipping platforms.
votes.concreteScenario: First-run provisioning (or Settings → reset protected data) runs ensureCommittedIfNeeded / bootstrapFirstDomainAfterAppAuthenticationIfNeeded, which derives the top-level wrapping-root KEK. The `let derivedWrappingRootKey` 32-byte buffer is deep-copied into SensitiveBytesBox and then released without zeroization when the function returns (only the box + provisionedSecret are zeroized). If an attacker later obtains a heap-disclosure primitive — a debug-entitled task_for_pid memory read, an unprotected macOS core dump, or an in-app use-after-free/out-of-bounds read — after the session has relocked (when the coordinator's live KEK and cached domain master keys are already zeroized), they can recover the freed KEK bytes. Because the HKDF salt/info are constants (ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:205-206, 220-222), the KEK alone lets them derive every domain wrapping key, unwrap all domain master keys from the Keychain-stored wrapped records, and decrypt private-key-control, protected-settings, key-metadata, and contacts-DB domains — bypassing the user-presence/biometric gate that normally guards the root secret. In practice this is mitigated by: MIE/MTE (blunts the UAF path), the one-time nature of provisioning (those buffers are usually overwritten quickly), and the fact that the same primitive during an unlocked session already yields the live key material directly.

findings.title: WCR-02 STILL OPEN — cypherair:// import parses untrusted input and presents its confirmation sheet over the lock surface while the app is locked
findings.location: Sources/App/Contacts/Import/IncomingURLImportCoordinator.swift:28-64 (handleIncomingURL — no lock gate); Sources/App/CypherAirApp.swift:461-463 (onOpenURL) and :372/:389 (ImportConfirmationSheetHost wraps content that also carries the lock overlay)
findings.defect: handleIncomingURL only guards on scheme, tutorial presentation, and a pending request — it never checks appLockController.isLocked. The router (AppSceneIncomingURLRouter.swift:9-15) likewise only checks the local-data-reset flag. On an incoming URL the app immediately runs the untrusted-input parser (PublicKeyImportLoader.loadFromURL → qrService.parseImportURL + inspectImportablePublicCertificate, FFI) and then presents ImportConfirmView via `.sheet`. Because that sheet is attached inside ImportConfirmationSheetHost to content() while AppLockSurfaceView is only an `.overlay` on that same content, the SwiftUI sheet renders modally ABOVE the opaque lock surface.
findings.invariant: Lock surface must gate interactive/attacker-controlled surfaces; untrusted input should not be processed pre-auth and no content should render above the lock screen.
findings.failureScenario: While the app is in the in-app locked state, a crafted cypherair:// URL (from another app/QR) drives the FFI certificate parser pre-authentication (attack surface exposure) and pops an import-confirmation sheet on top of the lock screen, letting a bystander/attacker import an untrusted contact key without unlocking.

votes.reason: The finding's load-bearing claims — an import-confirmation sheet with attacker metadata rendering over the lock surface, and a bystander importing an untrusted contact key without unlocking — are contradicted by the current code. handleIncomingURL (IncomingURLImportCoordinator.swift:44-60) presents the sheet only via importConfirmationCoordinator.present(request) at line 57, which is reached ONLY after makeImportConfirmationRequest succeeds. That call (ContactImportWorkflow.swift:14) synchronously invokes contactService.previewImportCandidateMatch, which guards `guard contactsAvailability == .availableProtectedDomain else { throw CypherAirError.contactsUnavailable(...) }` (ContactService.swift:102-105). Whenever the lock overlay is shown, the relock fan-out has already set contacts locked: AppLockController.enterRelock (runUnlockFlow:480 / enterLocked:570) -> relockProtectedData() -> ContactService.relockProtectedData() (ContactService.swift:907-912) -> clearContactsRuntimeState() sets `contactsAvailability = .locked`; on cold-launch `.locked` contacts were never opened. So while locked previewImportCandidateMatch throws, the throw is caught at handleIncomingURL line 61 (`importError = ...`), present(request) at line 57 is never reached, coordinator.request stays nil, and the ImportConfirmationSheetHost `.sheet` never presents — the user sees at most a generic app-defined 'Import Failed' alert with NO attacker-controlled content. Even if the sheet presented, importContactAfterConfirmation carries the identical `.availableProtectedDomain` guard (ContactService.swift:118-121), so no contact can be written while locked. The missing isLocked gate on the URL path is real but only exposes the Sequoia FFI parser pre-auth — memory-safe Rust with zeroize, pre-filtered by QRService.parseImportURL scheme/host/4096-byte checks (QRService.swift:49-73), and identical to the surface reachable when unlocked. That is a defense-in-depth nit, not the claimed confidentiality/integrity/custody defect. This matches the original WCR-02 (rated Low, medium confidence, z-order 'not runtime-observed', and explicitly noting the import write 'fails while locked').
votes.concreteScenario: Residual (info-level only): with the app at the lock surface, opening a crafted cypherair://import/v1/... URL runs QRService.parseImportURL -> FFI decodeQrUrl and validateImportablePublicCertificate pre-authentication, then previewImportCandidateMatch throws contactsUnavailable, surfacing a generic 'Import Failed' alert over the lock. No import-confirmation sheet, no attacker-metadata display, and no contact write occur while locked.

findings.title: WCR-04 STILL OPEN — crash between modify-expiry pending-bundle save and journal write orphans a usable SE-wrapped private-key copy
findings.location: Sources/Services/KeyManagement/KeyMutationService.swift:258-284 (saveBundle(.pending) at :259 precedes beginModifyExpiry journal at :280); recovery is journal-gated at Sources/Security/PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator.swift:19-23
findings.defect: modifyExpiry writes a complete wrapped private-key bundle into the .pending Keychain namespace (line 259-263) and only afterward calls privateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(...) to write the recovery journal (line 280). If the process dies in that window, the .pending bundle exists but no journal does. On next launch checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedRewrap early-returns when recoveryJournal() is nil, so the pending namespace is never inspected or cleaned, leaving an orphaned wrapped private-key copy indefinitely.
findings.invariant: Custody hygiene / crash-consistency: no orphaned duplicate copies of private-key material should persist untracked.
findings.failureScenario: App crashes (or is killed) right after the pending bundle is written but before beginModifyExpiry; the extra SE-wrapped private-key envelope lingers in the .pending Keychain service across launches, growing the private-key attack/artifact surface. It is still SE-wrapped (not plaintext) and is cleared by a full local-data reset, bounding severity.

votes.reason: The write/journal ordering is exactly as claimed. In KeyMutationService.performModifySoftwareExpiry, the complete SE-wrapped pending bundle is persisted first — `try bundleStore.saveBundle(bundle, fingerprint: fingerprint, namespace: .pending)` (lines 259-263) — and the recovery journal is only written afterward via `try privateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(fingerprint: fingerprint)` (line 280). `PrivateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry` (lines 337-342) sets `payload.recoveryJournal.modifyExpiry`, which is nil until that call. The catch blocks calling `cleanupPendingBundle` (264-267, 274-277) only fire on a thrown Swift error, not on an uncatchable process kill. On next launch, `checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedModifyExpiry()` (KeyMutationService:539-541) reads `recoveryJournal().modifyExpiry`; nil → returns nil, so the pending namespace is never inspected. `PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator.checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedRewrap` (:19-23) likewise returns nil without `rewrapTargetMode`. Grepping every `.pending`/`pendingPrivateKeyEnvelopeService` touch confirms there is no unconditional startup sweep — the only cleaners are journal-gated `recoverInterruptedMigration` (KeyMigrationCoordinator:118, which would delete a `(permanent .complete, pending .complete)` orphan IF it ran), key deletion (allPrivateKeychainServices includes the pending service, :576-581), and full reset. So the orphan mechanism is real. Note the finding mis-cites the rewrap coordinator as the gate; the actual modify-expiry gate is checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedModifyExpiry, but both are journal-gated so the substance is unchanged. Severity is genuinely low: the orphan is the same private key already present permanently, wrapped under a fresh SE key with the persisted mode's access control (identical protection tier — no plaintext, no weakened auth gate), the plaintext certData is zeroed at line 240, and it is cleared by key deletion, a re-run of modify-expiry, or local reset. No hard constraint (AEAD hard-fail, SE non-exportability, zeroing, logging, network) is breached.
votes.concreteScenario: 1) User with a software-custody key invokes change-expiry. 2) performModifySoftwareExpiry unwraps, modifies expiry, rewraps into a fresh SE-wrapped bundle, and at line 259 persists it to the pending Keychain service pendingPrivateKeyEnvelopeService(fingerprint). 3) The process is hard-killed (power loss, jetsam OOM, or force-quit) after line 259 completes but before line 280 (beginModifyExpiry) writes the modifyExpiry journal entry; the cleanupPendingBundle catch paths do not run on an uncatchable termination. 4) On next launch, checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedModifyExpiry() sees recoveryJournal().modifyExpiry == nil and returns nil, and checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedRewrap sees no rewrapTargetMode and returns nil — neither inspects the pending namespace. 5) The pending SE-wrapped private-key envelope persists untracked until the user deletes that key, re-runs modify-expiry on it (line 259 overwrites and the completing flow's promotePendingToPermanent cleans pending), or performs a full local-data reset. Impact is bounded: it is a duplicate of already-present key material at the same SE+access-control protection tier, exposing no plaintext and bypassing no authentication.

findings.title: Recipient selection does not filter unverifiable (CouldBe) subkey/cert revocations, unlike sibling local-key paths
findings.location: pgp-mobile/src/encrypt.rs:61 (cert-level gate) and encrypt.rs:75 / encrypt.rs:105 (.revoked(false) subkey filter)
findings.defect: The recipient-validation gate matches only RevocationStatus::Revoked(_) (encrypt.rs:61-68), and Sequoia's `.revoked(false)` filter (encrypt.rs:75, encrypt.rs:105; verified in sequoia-openpgp 2.4.0 src/cert/amalgamation/key/iter.rs:868-880) drops only RevocationStatus::Revoked(_) while keeping CouldBe. RevocationStatus::CouldBe arises when a subkey or cert carries a designated-revoker / third-party revocation the sender cannot cryptographically verify (revoker key absent). So such a subkey is still offered to Encryptor::for_recipients. This is asymmetric with the local-key mutation paths, which explicitly reject CouldBe: keys/expiry.rs:432, keys/revocation.rs:70, and cert_signature.rs:366. NOTE: this is most likely working-as-intended, not a true defect (see counter-argument in failureScenario). The core WCR-01 case — a live primary with a self/verifiable-Revoked encryption subkey — IS correctly filtered on every one of the nine encrypt/encrypt_binary/external-signer/streaming encrypt_file* entry points; this residual concerns only cryptographically unverifiable revocations.
findings.invariant: Confidentiality: do not encrypt to a recipient key the holder has revoked (WCR-01 recipient-revocation class).
findings.failureScenario: A recipient's cert is imported whose transport-encryption subkey carries a revocation issued by a designated revoker whose key the sender does not hold, so Sequoia evaluates revocation_status() = CouldBe. collect_recipients' cert-level check (matches only Revoked) passes and build_recipients' `.revoked(false)` retains the subkey; CypherAir encrypts to it. If that subkey was revoked due to compromise, the holder of the compromised private key can read the message. COUNTER-ARGUMENT (why this is likely correct-by-design): CouldBe means the revocation is unverifiable by this party; honoring it would let an attacker attach a forged/unverifiable revocation packet to a victim's cert to DENY encryption to the real recipient (a DoS + downgrade vector). Treating unverifiable revocations as non-binding is the standard OpenPGP/GnuPG default, and CypherAir's own nine key families do not issue designated-revoker revocations (only imported external certs could carry one). The asymmetry with the mutation paths is explainable: those rject CouldBe cheaply while re-signing the user's OWN cert, a different trust context. Net: real behavioral gap, marginal and defensible impact — surface for adjudication, not a confirmed vulnerability.

votes.reason:
The finding's TECHNICAL mechanism is fully confirmed from current code, but the security-defect characterization is refuted; the behavior is correct-by-design.

Confirmed facts:
- encrypt.rs:61-68 cert gate: `matches!(cert.revocation_status(policy, None), RevocationStatus::Revoked(_))` catches ONLY `Revoked`; `CouldBe`/`NotAsFarAsWeKnow` pass.
- Sequoia 2.4.0 `.revoked(false)` at .cargo/.../sequoia-openpgp-2.4.0/src/cert/amalgamation/key/iter.rs:868-882: line 869 skips only `RevocationStatus::Revoked(_)`; the `else` at 875-876 ("The key is probably not revoked") RETAINS `CouldBe`. So encrypt.rs:75 (collect) and :105 (build_recipients) keep CouldBe subkeys, feeding them to Encryptor::for_recipients on all 9 entry points.
- RevocationStatus enum (types/mod.rs:872+): `CouldBe` = "revocation certificate from a possible designated revoker" that cannot be cryptographically verified (revoker key absent).
- Asymmetry is real: expiry.rs:432, keys/revocation.rs:70, cert_signature.rs:366 all exhaustively REJECT CouldBe.
- CypherAir never mints designated-revoker certs (no revocation_key/revoker/set_revocation_keys in generation code); only externally imported certs could carry a CouldBe.

Why this is NOT a defect: Treating an UNVERIFIABLE revocation as binding is the non-standard, more-dangerous choice. OpenPGP/GnuPG default is to NOT honor a designated-revoker revocation when the revoker key is absent, precisely because honoring it lets an attacker staple a forged, unverifiable revocation packet onto a victim's cert to DENY encryption to the legitimate recipient (DoS/downgrade). Sequoia's enum doc even flags the DoS direction. The WCR-01 invariant is about VERIFIABLE holder self-revocations (→ `Revoked`), which every encrypt path still filters correctly. The mutation-path asymmetry is a distinct trust context (conservatively refusing to re-sign the user's OWN cert under unresolved status, which has no DoS downside). The prior pass itself conceded "most likely working-as-intended."
votes.concreteScenario: No genuine exploit. The only theoretical confidentiality path requires: (a) the recipient themselves configured a designated revoker (authenticated in a self-sig), (b) a revocation purportedly from that revoker exists on the imported cert, (c) the sender does NOT hold the revoker's key (→ CouldBe), (d) the revocation was genuinely a compromise revocation, and (e) the compromiser intercepts the ciphertext. Under these narrow, unverifiable, self-inflicted conditions CypherAir would still encrypt. But honoring CouldBe instead would introduce a strictly worse, trivially-reachable DoS/downgrade vector (anyone can attach an unverifiable revocation packet), which is why mainstream OpenPGP implementations deliberately do exactly what CypherAir does. Net: defensible standard behavior, not a vulnerability.

findings.title: 32-byte KEM/ECDH key-share copy handed to UniFFI at the callback-return boundary is never zeroized (inherent, documented-accepted)
findings.defect: Each key-agreement bridge zeroizes its local secret (`keyShare`/`raw`) via `defer`, but the final copy that is handed to UniFFI as the return record — `let ffiShare = keyShare.withUnsafeBytes { Data(buffer) }; return MlKem768KeyShare(raw: ffiShare)` (and the P256 analog `ffiRaw`) — is not and cannot be zeroized, because it must be alive when UniFFI lowers it. UniFFI then serializes it into a transient RustBuffer that is also not scrubbed before free. The result is that a 32-byte ML-KEM/ECDH shared key-share (one combiner input of the RFC 9980 KEM) transiently persists in unzeroed Swift heap and the RustBuffer beyond the bridge scope, a partial deviation from the 'all key buffers zeroed on all paths' invariant. Rust immediately re-wraps the lifted Vec in `Zeroizing`/`Protected`, so the durable copy is scrubbed; only the boundary copies linger. The P256 bridge documents this explicitly (lines 29-30); the ML-KEM 768/1024 bridges do the same thing without the comment.
findings.failureScenario: An attacker with a post-decryption heap-disclosure primitive (e.g. an uninitialized-read/heap-spray info-leak in another component) reads freed-but-unscrubbed heap and recovers a 32-byte ML-KEM/ECDH key share for a single message. This is a combiner input, not a long-term private key (the enclave key never leaves hardware) and does not by itself yield the session key; exposure is bounded by short lifetime, ASLR, and hardware memory tagging (MIE). It is inherent to UniFFI by-value record returns — there is no code path that can zero a buffer after it is returned — so this is best treated as an accepted, already-mitigated limitation rather than a fixable defect.
findings.invariant: Invariant #4: all key/passphrase/plaintext buffers zeroed on all paths

votes.reason: The described behavior is real and precisely reproduced in current code, but it is an inherent, documented-accepted, hardware-mitigated limitation rather than a fixable defect. In each bridge the enclave secret's local buffer IS scrubbed, but a fresh return copy is not: PGPExternalMlKem768DecapsulationProviderBridge.swift:26-28 — `defer { keyShare.resetBytes(in: 0..<keyShare.count) }` then `let ffiShare = keyShare.withUnsafeBytes { buffer in Data(buffer) }` / `return MlKem768KeyShare(raw: ffiShare)`; the 1024 bridge is byte-identical (:26-28); the P256 bridge does the same with `ffiRaw` (:26-31) and explicitly documents it at lines 29-30 ("UniFFI must copy this record across the callback boundary; Rust immediately validates and stores the received Vec in Zeroizing"). The generated lowering confirms the copy is serialized unscrubbed: pgp_mobile.swift:1365 `writeReturn = { uniffiOutReturn.pointee = FfiConverterTypeMlKem768KeyShare_lower($0) }` -> :5434 `FfiConverterData.write(value.raw, into: &buf)`, producing a RustBuffer that is not scrubbed before free. The bridge cannot scrub `ffiShare`/`ffiRaw` because it must be alive at `return`, and the RustBuffer is created inside generated bindings that must not be hand-edited — so it is genuinely inherent to UniFFI by-value record returns, and applies pervasively to all secret material crossing the boundary, not just these three files. The Rust durable copy is re-wrapped/zeroized (external_composite_decryptor.rs:261 `ExternalMlKem768Share::new(response.raw)`; record docs keys.rs:195 and pgp_mobile.swift:5400). Only crown-jewel invariant #4 (all buffers zeroed on all paths) is technically/partially deviated, for a copy no code path can scrub; invariants #1 (AEAD hard-fail), #3 (SE non-exportability — the private key never leaves hardware; only a derived per-message value transiently copies), and the rest are intact. The one concrete, actionable gap is documentation consistency: the two ML-KEM bridges omit the explanatory comment the P256 bridge carries.
votes.concreteScenario: An attacker who already holds an independent post-decryption heap-disclosure primitive (e.g. an uninitialized-read/info-leak in another component) reads freed-but-unscrubbed Swift heap or the transient RustBuffer and recovers the 32-byte value for ONE message: for the ML-KEM bridges this is a single RFC 9980 combiner input (the classical X25519/X448 share, the combiner, and AES key-unwrap remain in Rust's Zeroizing, so it is insufficient alone to derive the session key); for the P256 bridge it is that message's raw ECDH shared secret, which yields at most that one message's session key via the OpenPGP ECDH KDF but never the Secure-Enclave private key (non-exportable). The window is narrow and transient, and success also requires defeating ASLR and the mandatory MIE/hardware memory-tagging entitlement (constraint #8). Blast radius is bounded to a single message; no private key, no partial plaintext, no long-term secret is exposed. This is inherent to UniFFI record returns and not fixable in app code, so it is an accepted, already-mitigated limitation.

findings.title: WCR-05 STILL OPEN — Encrypt-to-Self picker selection never re-validated; stale/deleted self-key silently falls back to default key
findings.defect: resolvedEncryptToSelfKey looks up the chosen encryptToSelfFingerprint via keyManagement.keys.first(where:); if that fingerprint no longer matches any key (deleted/rotated after selection) it silently `else if let defaultKey` — falling back to keyManagement.defaultKey with no error and no UI signal. The selection is never re-validated at send time.
findings.invariant: User-intent fidelity for self-recipient selection (not a confidentiality boundary — every candidate is one of the user's own keys).
findings.failureScenario: User selects self-key A (e.g. held on a specific device) then deletes/rotates A; a later encrypt silently attaches the self-copy to default key B instead. The outgoing self-copy is still only decryptable by the user's own keys, so this is an availability/expectation mismatch (the device holding A can no longer read it) rather than a confidentiality or integrity breach; hence info-level.

votes.reason: The described behavior is real in the current code. EncryptionService.swift:196-203 resolvedEncryptToSelfKey: `if let fp = encryptToSelfFingerprint, let key = keyManagement.keys.first(where: { $0.fingerprint == fp }) { return key.publicKeyData } else if let defaultKey = keyManagement.defaultKey { return defaultKey.publicKeyData } else { throw CypherAirError.noKeySelected }`. A stale/unmatched fingerprint silently falls into the default-key branch with no error and no UI signal, and the selection is never re-validated at send time. Reachable: encryptToSelfFingerprint is set from the picker (EncryptOptionsSection.swift:19-28 over model.ownKeys = keyManagement.keys, EncryptScreenModel.swift:254-256) or from applyInitialSignerSelection (EncryptScreenModel.swift:889-893); SwiftUI Picker does not reset a bound value whose tag disappears, so deleting the selected self-key leaves a stale fingerprint. HOWEVER this is NOT a security defect: both branches return .publicKeyData of a keyManagement.keys entry, and defaultKey is keys.first(where: \.isDefault) (KeyManagementService.swift:503-505) — every candidate AND the fallback are the user's OWN keys. No path encrypts the self-copy to a non-self key, so there is zero confidentiality/integrity/authentication/custody/memory impact. It reads only public key material. The fallback is a reasonable defensive default that preserves the encrypt-to-self guarantee; the recipient path (EncryptScreenModel.swift:266-271) reserves stale-selection surfacing for the confidentiality-relevant recipient set. Confirmed as accurate behavior, but info severity only — a user-intent/availability nit, exactly as the finding author self-rated and disclaimed ("not a confidentiality or integrity breach").
votes.concreteScenario: User has ≥2 self keys, opens Encrypt, picks self-key A in the "Encrypt to Self With" picker (encryptToSelfFingerprint = A's fp). User then deletes/rotates key A in key management. The Picker's stale-value binding retains A's fingerprint. On the next encrypt, resolvedEncryptToSelfKey finds no key matching A, falls through to defaultKey B, and attaches the self-copy under B with no warning. The device that held only key A can no longer read that self-copy; a device holding default key B still can. Availability/expectation mismatch only — the self-copy remains decryptable exclusively by the user's own keys, so no confidentiality, integrity, or authentication boundary is crossed.

completeness.overall: The deep-pass covered the in-memory decrypt DoS/zeroize surface, cert-signature policy, KDF-on-SKESK, composite/split-custody decrypt, and per-site Swift/Rust zeroize hygiene well. The most significant blind spots are (1) the FILE decrypt path as a first-class attacker surface — the panel examined streaming.rs only for PKESK accumulation and treated the temp-file+rename machinery as fully sufficient, but never checked decompressed-output bounding, so the confirmed in-memory decompression bomb has an unflagged disk-fill twin; (2) the concurrency model of the ENTIRE ProtectedData layer, not just the one dictionary the contested finding names — the stores are `@unchecked Sendable`, `relockProtectedData()` is a non-isolated `async` method that hops off the main actor, and the shared `ProtectedDomainKeyManager` plus `ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.wrappingRootKey` have zero internal locking, so relock-vs-in-flight-decrypt is a structurally real data race the majority appears to have refuted by assuming (not verifying) main-thread confinement; and (3) the signing/detached-verify paths and the split-custody KEM error/timing-distinguishability were only shallowly touched. Randomness (invariant #5), format auto-selection (#2, Sequoia-delegated + cross-profile packet tests), non-exportability via export_secret_key (Sequoia errors when a cert has no secret parts, so SE/split-custody certs cannot be exported), merge_public_certificate_update (fingerprint-match + Sequoia self-sig canonicalization blocks subkey injection), and the S2K left-shift (argon2-crate m_cost bounds make Sequoia reject out-of-range params, so the 5/5 refutation holds save a latent non-shipped debug panic) all held up under spot checks and are NOT gaps.

gaps.area: Untrusted-parsing DoS on the FILE decrypt path (decompression/disk-fill bomb)
gaps.notExamined: pgp-mobile/src/streaming.rs:660 (decrypt_file_with_helper streams the decryptor+decompressor through zeroing_copy into the temp file with NO output-size cap). The confirmed in-memory decompression-bomb finding (decrypt.rs:285) has a direct file-path analog that was never flagged: a small encrypted file wrapping a high-ratio compressed literal packet expands unbounded into `${output}.<rand>.tmp`. Sources/Services/DiskSpaceChecker.swift:validateForEncryption only guards the ENCRYPTION path against inputFileSize*2 and is input-size-based; there is no decryption-output disk guard at all. Same uncapped Sequoia Decryptor the panel already agreed does not cap decompression.
gaps.suggestedProbe: Build an OpenPGP message: literal data of e.g. 4 GB of zeros -> ZIP/ZLIB compression packet -> SEIPD to a test key; write the (tiny) ciphertext to a file and run the file-decrypt path. Observe the temp file growing to fill the volume before any AEAD tag check at EOF; on iOS confirm Jetsam/other-app impact, on macOS confirm volume exhaustion. Compare to the in-memory finding to confirm parity. Fix direction: bound decrypted-output bytes (abort past a ceiling) on both decrypt paths.

gaps.area: Concurrency / data race across the whole ProtectedData custody layer (memory-safety + key-material integrity)
gaps.notExamined: The panel isolated ONE symptom (unlockedDomainMasterKeys dict) and refuted a sibling (wrappingRootKey torn read) but never audited the layer's synchronization model as a whole. KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:5, PrivateKeyControlStore.swift:6, ProtectedSettingsStore.swift:6 are all `final class ... @unchecked Sendable` (an unaudited, compiler-unenforced thread-safety assertion). `relockProtectedData() async throws` (ProtectedDataRelockParticipant) is NOT actor/@MainActor isolated, so `await participant.relockProtectedData()` in ProtectedDataSessionRelockCoordinator runs on the generic executor, off main. The shared ProtectedDomainKeyManager (plain final class) mutates unlockedDomainMasterKeys in clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys() (relock) and reads it in unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:) (domain access during decrypt/key-load, KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift:462) with no lock/queue/actor. ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.wrappingRootKey (non-atomic Data?) is read at :197-202 while zeroized off-main at :143/:229-235. Nothing in these TYPES enforces that relock cannot overlap an in-flight domain access — the invariant is assumed at a higher layer, not guaranteed.
gaps.suggestedProbe: Run the CypherAir-UnitTests / device lane under ThreadSanitizer while driving a background-triggered relock (scenePhase away) concurrently with an in-flight decrypt or key-metadata load that reads a DMK. Alternatively, static-audit every `@unchecked Sendable` in Sources/Security/ProtectedData for the actual serialization guarantee. Worst case: a Swift Dictionary concurrent mutate+read = heap corruption / crash, or a decrypt reads a half-zeroized DMK/wrapping-root-key (use-after-zeroize) — both memory-safety crown-jewel violations. See contestedWorthReopening.

gaps.area: Split-custody PQC decapsulation: CCA / error-and-timing distinguishability (decryption oracle)
gaps.notExamined: pgp-mobile/src/external_composite_decryptor/core.rs:122-131 classifies InvalidResponse / ClassicalComponentFailure / ExternalFailure as fail-closed (good), and the doc notes 'classical-half-first ordering'. The custody-splitpqc lens examined the wildcard hard-abort but NOT whether, for a TARGETED (non-wildcard) composite PKESK, a malformed classical half fails fast (before the ML-KEM SE decapsulation is ever exercised) versus a valid classical half + wrong KEM share — a timing/branch oracle over attacker-chosen ciphertext components. ML-KEM is IND-CCA2 with implicit rejection, but the COMBINER's early-exit ordering and whether distinct error variants survive to the FFI boundary (vs collapsing to one opaque CypherAirError) was not verified.
gaps.suggestedProbe: Feed a matrix of composite PKESKs: (valid classical half + malformed KEM share) vs (malformed classical half + valid KEM share) vs both-valid-wrong; measure wall-clock to failure and inspect the surfaced error at the Swift boundary for any distinguisher. Confirm the ML-KEM half is always run (constant-time w.r.t. classical validity) or that no observable channel differentiates the failure modes. If a distinguisher exists, assess exploitability against the split-custody confidentiality guarantee.

gaps.notExamined: pgp-mobile/src/sign.rs (sign_cleartext, sign_detached_with_signer, and the external-P256/composite signer variants at :64/:82/:104) and pgp-mobile/src/external_composite_signer/* received no lens. Not checked: (a) that signing selects the intended live/unrevoked signing key and cannot be steered to a wrong/expired subkey (the :35 comment hints at multi-signing-key selection nuance); (b) detached-signature verification robustness over a large attacker-controlled data file (streaming verify) for DoS or truncation-attribution issues; (c) whether the external composite signer can be induced to sign attacker-chosen digests it shouldn't (signing-oracle at the SE boundary).
gaps.suggestedProbe: Audit select_external_signing_key (encrypt.rs:168 region) and sign.rs key-selection for revoked/expired/wrong-capability subkey selection with a multi-subkey cert. Fuzz detached-verify with oversized and truncated data inputs. Trace the external composite signer callback to confirm the digest it signs is bound to app-originated content only, not attacker-supplied.

gaps.notExamined: pgp-mobile/src/armor.rs:decode_armor uses ReaderMode::Tolerant and read_to_end with no size cap on untrusted armored input (clipboard/file/.asc). Bounded only ~1.33x by input length so not a true amplification bomb, but it was not examined alongside the DoS lens and combines with the uncapped packet/decompression parsing downstream.
gaps.suggestedProbe: Feed multi-hundred-MB armored blobs and armored-then-compressed payloads through the dearmor->parse pipeline; confirm memory stays bounded and no amplification chains with cert/message parsing. Low priority relative to the file-decrypt disk-fill gap.

gaps.area: Non-exportability invariant (#3) for SE-custody and split-custody keys — verify, don't assume
gaps.notExamined: export_secret_key (secret_transfer.rs:69) relies on parts_into_secret()/take_secret() erroring when a cert carries no secret packets, which is the implicit guard that stops SE / split-custody certs (public cert + handle only) from being serialized with private material. This was inferred, not directly probed. Not verified: that composite_custody_generation / secure_enclave_generation never leave any secret packet in the serialized cert, and that no other serialization path (backup, self-test at SelfTestService.swift:325, key-metadata persistence) can emit the software-held half of a split-custody key.
gaps.suggestedProbe: For each of the four device-bound / split-custody families, generate a key, then attempt exportSecretKey and inspect the serialized cert bytes (is_tsk / has any SecretKeyMaterial packet) to confirm zero private material is ever emitted. Add a negative assertion only if a real path is found (per repo's anti-box-checking testing mandate).

contestedWorthReopening: Unsynchronized shared Dictionary of decrypted domain master keys (ProtectedDomainKeyManager.swift:186; mutators :338/:346; reader :342) — REOPEN as high. The majority (4/5) refuted it, but the refutation appears to rest on assumed main-thread confinement that the code does NOT enforce: ProtectedDomainKeyManager is a plain `final class` with no lock/queue/actor, its consumer stores are `@unchecked Sendable` (compiler-unchecked assertion), and `relockProtectedData() async` is non-isolated so it executes off the main actor. clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys() (relock) and unlockedDomainMasterKey(for:) (domain access during a decrypt/key-load) can therefore touch the same non-thread-safe Swift Dictionary concurrently when the user backgrounds the app mid-operation. That is a genuine data race: heap corruption/crash at best, and at worst a decrypt proceeding against a concurrently-zeroized domain master key — a memory-safety AND key-integrity crown-jewel violation. Its refuted sibling, the wrappingRootKey torn read in ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator (:16 / :197-202 read vs :143/:229-235 off-main zeroize), shares the identical root cause and should be re-triaged in the same pass rather than dismissed independently.
